> **Jack Kruse said**
> I cant stress to you enough furface how much the Dec 2012 Webinar will light you on fire...........

I can't wait to read/listen to it, Dr. Kruse. Can't wait until it's posted. I love how I'm feeling right now. I'm also trying to rid myself, and my surroundings of any negativity, as in my experience that can really bring one down, and put an immediate halt to any progress...physically or mentally that a person has made, or will make, if they choose to let it. Which at times can be extremely difficult not to let happen.

A positive attitude usually translates into positive results. A negative attitude usually translates into negative results. You must believe in yourself first before anyone else can, or will.

Before you know it you are going to be walking around like me - can't wipe the silly grin off my face! That certainly doesn't mean that all the bad/sad things have gone away - only that you are just so much more positive and just plain darn glad to be alive!

### furface — 2012-12-13T17:21:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-67389>

That's awesome, Caroline! Good for you! I can't wait either. I have a natural smile, or smirk on my face all the time as it is...at least then I'll have an actual reason for it to be there. LOL!

Just watched a youtube video on a very, very simple recipe for cooking swordfish...something I've always wanted to try. When I get paid next week, I'm hoping that one of our gazillion seafood shops here in Vancouver has some for sale, so I can test out that recipe.

Had my third face dunking session this morning, and got up to 35 seconds for one of my 6 dunks. Averaged 32.5 for all 6. I love it!

Do many people on this journey eat tomatoes, or bell peppers? Just curious as I love them, but a bit confused about the whole nightshades stuff.

### caroline — 2012-12-13T18:39:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-67392>

I didn't realize that you live in Vancouver - such a gorgeous city! You have such fabulous restaurants there. I lived in Canada most of my life.
About the night shades ......I personally kinda dropped them naturally. I will sometimes have some but I think I feel better without them. I think I probably have always had low level sensitivities. I have recently dropped eggs and feel better. Once again - it is such a personal journey.
I have a personal question/observation - I hope this isn't out of line completely - just ignore me if it is! I hope when you get more optimum you will change your forum handle to something much more positive and flattering that reflects your happy personality and your lean healthy body!

### furface — 2012-12-13T18:57:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-67395>

LOL, I'll think about the name change. But one reason why I chose "Furface" is because by trade, I'm a nature photographer, and a lot of nature photographers grow beards...helps keep the face warm when we're working outdoors in colder weather. Photography makes me smile, it's something I love, and unfortunately right now I am unable to do much of it for lack of resources, and equipment. But it's a reminder of a true passion of mine.

Â 

Yeah, Vancouver is a beautiful city, although I really don't eat in fancy restaurants all that much. The "KEG" is about as fancy as I've been. haha.

That's so cool that you've lived all over Canada. So have I actually, but I always come home to Vancouver. (Well I currently live in Surrey, which is a suburb basically of Vancouver. So same thing essentially. lol)

Â 

Thanks for your input on nightshades etc. It's not that I'd stuff my face with them constantly, but just for a variety, and a change from eating basically only GREEN vegetables. It would be nice to add some COLOUR to a meal once in a while. Also, I like to make homemade in the pan, on the spot, "tomato" sauce once in a while, esp with shrimp. Toss some garlic and onions into the pan along with some butter, or coconut oil...perhaps some EVOO...although I really hate the taste of Extra Virgin Olive Oil, add some cut up tomatoes, or strawberry tomatoes, spices, let them cook down, and you have a nice, tasty, kind of thickened sauce for your shrimp, or fish etc.

I don't think I'd ever cut out eggs though. I love omelettes, they are a quick way to add a dozen or so grams of protein to the BAB, with a couple of cackleberries, and if you get free run, or free range eggs, they have such deep, golden yolks. Not saying I'll eat them by the dozen each week, but they're a nice "fall back", or "safety valve" source of protein.

### PrimalPam — 2012-12-13T19:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-67398>

Re:Â  Robb Wolf talking about lean protein:Â  Robb himself has said that when he originally wrote his book and started Paleo, heÂ was trying to draw in people who were eating all versions of the SAD diet and vegetarians.Â  Typically, people are so ingrained in LowFat as a way of life that Robb didn't want to immediately alienate and turn off people.Â  He wanted to get his main point across:Â  That we have been around for millions of years eating animal protein, yet wheat has only been around for a few thousand.Â  Get off wheat.Â  Eat animals.Â  So he said Lowfat protein to just GET people to eat protein because he was afraid if he started with saturated fat, people would automatically turn away.Â 

Â 

Most people in the paleosphere do talk about the importance of fat in the diet.Â  Where they seem to stop is that eating meat is the protein mainstay; they do not seem to associate the importance of DHA via seafood.Â

### caroline — 2012-12-13T19:32:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-67399>

I know - I always loved eggs and ate a lot of them. Omelettes used to be my absolute favorite but I feel intuitively that I shouldn't be eating them - as much anyway. I ate them alot when I was travelling because it was the most available protein source. I am just going to cut them out for awhile and then maybe re introduce them later if I want.
Too bad you can't get back to photography. Doing happy stuff is so much better for us - being passionate about something makes all the difference - even better if you can earn a living doing it - perfect combination for an optimum life.

### furface — 2012-12-13T19:54:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-67405>

> **PrimalPam said**
> Re:Â  Robb Wolf talking about lean protein:Â  Robb himself has said that when he originally wrote his book and started Paleo, heÂ was trying to draw in people who were eating all versions of the SAD diet and vegetarians.Â  Typically, people are so ingrained in LowFat as a way of life that Robb didn't want to immediately alienate and turn off people.Â  He wanted to get his main point across:Â  That we have been around for millions of years eating animal protein, yet wheat has only been around for a few thousand.Â  Get off wheat.Â  Eat animals.Â  So he said Lowfat protein to just GET people to eat protein because he was afraid if he started with saturated fat, people would automatically turn away.Â 
>
> Â 
>
> Most people in the paleosphere do talk about the importance of fat in the diet.Â  Where they seem to stop is that eating meat is the protein mainstay; they do not seem to associate the importance of DHA via seafood.Â 

Thanks, Pam. Oh I love seafood, don't get me wrong, and intend to eat a great deal of it. It will most likely make up at least 50-60% of my weekly protein quotient...if not more. So many varieties. Don't know if clams and oysters will be a huge part of that...just being honest, but they will find a way into my diet, I promise.

As I mentioned earlier, I am planning on buying some swordfish, hopefully some dungeness crab, lobster, lots of prawns, some salmon, sablefish, snapper, halibut, basa, tilapia etc. as well. Of course bacon for my BAB, and yes some grass-fed ground beef, grass-fed lamb, hormone free pork possibly, and the other day I found a local butcher that had GF beef short ribs, and bought a few...OMG mouthwatering, and only $3.99 per lb.

So I will be getting plenty of DHA hopefully, and will continue along this most wonderful pathway for as long as I can.

### furface — 2012-12-13T20:01:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-67409>

> **caroline said**
> I know - I always loved eggs and ate a lot of them. Omelettes used to be my absolute favorite but I feel intuitively that I shouldn't be eating them - as much anyway. I ate them alot when I was travelling because it was the most available protein source. I am just going to cut them out for awhile and then maybe re introduce them later if I want.
> Too bad you can't get back to photography. Doing happy stuff is so much better for us - being passionate about something makes all the difference - even better if you can earn a living doing it - perfect combination for an optimum life.

Oh I understand fully where you're coming from regarding the eggs. As you put it so eloquently before...it's all about each individual's own choices, and feelings about this.

Oh I'll get back into photography eventually. It's just that the equipment I want...yes want, not need, but want is rather expensive, and I'm not the type of person to "settle" for something less that will just tide me over until I can afford what I want. I find that to be wasting money since I wouldn't really be happy with less than optimal equipment, and I'd just have to spend even more later to get what I want, so I may as well save up a little longer until I can afford what I really want. I'm sorry if that sounds conceited, but when you've used the best, "settling" for inferior equipment, or whatever just doesn't measure up.

I agree fully with you when you say that doing happy stuff is so much better, and being passionate about something makes all the difference. You can't wait to go out and do whatever it is you're passionate about. Whether it's photography, or art, or mountain climbing, or singing, or LeptinRx, CT, this whole journey, traveling, whatever.

### Christina Gagnon — 2018-08-03T00:53:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/confused-a-bit-about-something.5141/post-247333>

[@furface](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5445/) We belong to our local CSA and I have a few small potatoes - they are local and it is Summer - I wonder if I could get away with eating a few?

> [furface said:](/goto/post?id=67304)
>
> Thanks again, Vkiernan. You make great points.
>
> I have read many of the blogs, I believe I've read the before LS, and after LS ones, although to be totally honest, I find the length of them at times a bit distracting, and wish he would get to the point a bit quicker. haha. No offense meant of course, but as I've mentioned before, I like taking the direct route, not the scenic route most of the time, and some of the techno babble can get confusing, and makes me lose interest in what I'm reading. But I will reread them.
>
> Â
>
> Another great point you make is about not HAVING to drown each meal in fat. I guess I'm just thinking that since it's been mentioned that fat should be the largest component of the three macronutrients in each meal, then protein, and miniscule amount of carbs, then if my BAB for instance contains say 60 grams of carbs, then if fat should make up 70-75 % of the macronutrient total, the meal should contain over 80 grams of healthy fats. (Sorry if my math is off, but I don't feel like figuring out the exact numbers right now. lol) As I said, I'm just trying to "follow orders".
>
> Â
>
> As I've mentioned, I began this a week and a half ago, which was about a week and a half since I last got paid (I get paid once per month), so my freezer was pretty much stocked up with what I had bought previously. Some grass-fed lean ground beef, which I formed into burgers before freezing them individually, quite a bit of lean ground turkey, which I also preformed into burgers, some salmon, tilapia, and a few individually wrapped pieces of pork shoulder butt steaks. (Boneless).Â I don't like to waste food, and don't know anyone to give some of it away too...plus if I did, I wouldn't have anything to eat, so I've been "cleaning" out my freezer, while trying to add some of the better choices when I've come into a few dollars before payday.
>
> I've bought a couple of bags of frozen prawns, a can of crabmeat (meh it was okay) a can of smoked oysters (tasted like dirt), 3 nice big fillets of Basa (white roughy), and lots of bacon for my BAB, along with some free-run eggs. I've also managed to get a grass-fed lamb chop, and some grass-fed beef short ribs...OMG sooo good.
>
> When I get paid next week, I'm planning on buying plenty more basa, some salmon, bacon, GF ground beef, and short ribs, prawns, clams, I might even try some fresh oysters. Hopefully I'll even be able to afford some fresh dungeness crab, and esp some fresh Lobster. As well as free run eggs, coconut oil...which I also bought a small jar of last week, some kerrygold butter, heavy whipping cream to make a nice clam chowder. (Don't worry, no potatoes...I hate potatoes in soups or stews etc) Some 85, 90, 99 % dark chocolate. Baby bok choy, asparagus, long english cucumbers, and even some sea veggies if I can find some. Might even pick up some pistachios and macadamia nuts to have a FEW as part of a meal...just for a change of pace.
>
> So I have a good idea of what to get, I'm just waiting to be able to get it. haha.
>
> I'm actually surprised that it really hasn't been difficult to eliminate snacks. Also, my blood sugar levels have been fantastic, and that also inspires me.
>
> I'm going to give this my best effort, and so far I'm liking it very much.
>
> I also appreciate all the help and support the members of this board have given me, and wish you, and everyone else continued best of luck, and success.

# Thread: People irritate me...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/people-irritate-me.5146/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-12T21:59:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/people-irritate-me.5146/post-67291>

So, I typed up a goofy little post ( http://intoxicatedonlife.com/2012/12/11/whole-foods-diet-for-family/) on my blog showing the differences in reactions towards my dietary choices for my kids I've received from a couple of different doctors I've had to take my twins to for periodic fevers. Anyhow, I thought it was just a fun post and linked to it on my Facebook not expecting anyone to say anything much, but of course I was wrong. I don't know why I'm so irritated by the comment. Maybe it's because I can hear the condescension in this persons voice since I know them IRL. Alright, rant over...here is what was said.

"I respect your diet decisions. Your dedication to healthy diet is amazing! It will be interesting to see what happens when they encounter the items that aren't included in their diet since acclimation, as you know, is part of the mechanics of the digestive system. Without exposure, they could present as being "allergic" to some items that they just weren't exposed to even though no allergy exists...Celiac Disease, a reaction to the gluten protein in wheat, affects about 1 in 2500 individuals in the United States and these type of diets are a reaction to public concern fed by the marketing departments of health food companies and the dieticians/doctors that support them. I don't see the need for this type of diet unless there is presentation of Celiac Disease, but respect those who attempt it. It has to be challenging."

### DarleenMB — 2012-12-12T22:41:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/people-irritate-me.5146/post-67292>

Send your "friend" a link to Dr. Davis's website. Â www.wheatbellyblog.com

Then sit back and grin.

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-13T10:23:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/people-irritate-me.5146/post-67347>

I suppose now I just need to write a post on why I don't eat wheat...

As I think about it, he was assuming it was a bad thing if later in my life my kids eat garbage food and have a bad reaction to it. In my. Mind, its probably a good thing since they most likely wouldn't be anxious to eat it again soon given a bad reaction....right?

### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-13T15:41:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/people-irritate-me.5146/post-67378>

Oh shoot...I tried to explain about wheat and the different anti nutrients in wheat and now I have more people that don't like what I have to say:
Gluten isn't an anti-nutrient; it's a protein used by wheat both for holding the grain together and for providing most of the initial protein matter for the newly growing plant. Even ancient wheat had gluten, along with other related grasses which we don't typically consume. Although I don't know the mechanism, it's these protein strands which our immune system gets confused by in people with wheat allergies -- as well as peanuts, cat dander (that's me), or even poison ivy. If your body does have a reaction to gluten, then it will cause swelling throughout the body. If your body does not, then it does not -- you can safely consume the protein to your heart's (stomach's) content.

Â Do not let them drag you to a place you have visited briefly in your past........It is OK to hate me for saying it, because it is cutting........To conquer frustration, one must remain intensely focused on the outcome, not the obstacles we face. ### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-12-13T22:39:42+0200

Being artificial about something, anything, Â creates a block inside of us. Life canâ€™t work for us if we donâ€™t show up as we are created. No, I don't hate you for saying it. I know my frustration is totally unfounded. I think thats why I vented it on here. I certainly didn't want to vent it on facebook. ### vkiernan — 2012-12-14T08:44:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/people-irritate-me.5146/post-67480>

> **TrishaL. said
>
> > Jack Kruse said
>
> **
>
> > Trisha.......you're speaking from frustration.......you are better than that. Â Anger and frustration are the result of us not being authentic somewhere in our life or with someone in our life. Being artificial about something, anything, Â creates a block inside of us. Life canâ€™t work for us if we donâ€™t show up as we are created. Â Do not let them drag you to a place you have visited briefly in your past........It is OK to hate me for saying it, because it is cutting........To conquer frustration, one must remain intensely focused on the outcome, not the obstacles we face. Â 
>
> No, I don't hate you for saying it. I know my frustration is totally unfounded. I think thats why I vented it on here. I certainly didn't want to vent it on facebook. When I think about it with my head screwed on straight I realize that all a lot of people is the food pyramid and nutrition as it's been taught for years in this country, so why should they believe that anything but a grain based diet is best?Â 
>
> Thanks for the reminder... eyes on the goal!

Trisha,Â if I'm reading this right, I don't think Jack is saying you can hate him, I think he is referring to the people reading your blogs and such.Â  He is telling you to stick firm to your beliefs and not let others intimidate or distract you from them.Â  I think his blog that was resurrected would be a very good read for you today if you have not read it.Â  It was about the primal spirit and 12 wishes iirc.Â  It was a huge reminder for me on all aspects of enjoying life.Â  I have people telling me all the time, oh in moderation or just a little won't hurt.Â  Actually, in my case it does hurt and there is no moderation.Â  Don't get frustrated over other's beliefs.Â  That is theirs to own and not yours.Â  You have yours and need to stick to them and feel great about it otherwise do you truly own the belief or the want ofÂ friendship from others?Â Â  Like Jack mentioned in the blog, people will like you no matter what if you are congruent in your beliefs.Â Â  They will no where you stand and appreciate that value in you.Â  It doesn't matter if they agree with you or not.Â  That is not a reason to like or dislike someone.Â  We all have different beliefs on many different levels.Â  You keep doing the good things you are doing and feel fantastic about them because you know it is the right thing.Â  Learn to agree to disagree and accept others for who they are!

Loved this. I've been elevating my feet for yrs since returning from Asia. I use anything currently I use Chaos & Havoc's stool so they can reach the potty. I taught them they need to support their feet when they go potty.

### maggie — 2012-12-13T13:20:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-help-your-doc-understand-the-links-between-gut-and-spine.5149/post-67359>

Jack, when I read your blog this a.m., I thought Holy Crap!Â  \[pun intended\]Â  Jack just wrote the history of my family.Â  Seriously.Â  I had acute appendicitis at age 68... wtf, thought I, what bad luck.Â  So far in my generation, we've got everything except DDD, but with my recent joint issues, I'd imagine that might not be far behind.Â  Looking at the collocation of diseases among acquaintances of my age cohort, the big picture starts to come into focus.

What I love about the blogs now is that they give a basis for intuitive choices which helps reinforce those good decisions.Â  I had a squatty potty for years--after experiencing this practice in Asia and the ME--seemed more natural and healthy.Â  Eventually I threw it away because family members were making snotty comments, and I just didn't realize its importance.Â  I've been thinking about taking an Iyengar yoga class after the holidays.Â  After reading this blog and getting a sense of the importance of body alignment, I'm going to the studio this morning to sign up!Â  When you know better, etc etc.Â 

Can't wait for the December webinar.Â  Any sense of when it might be available?

Â 

Thanks again and again for sharing your wisdom.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-08-12T22:17:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-help-your-doc-understand-the-links-between-gut-and-spine.5149/post-199567>

Spine surgeons, Chiropractors, and Primary care doctors better start learning about light. The intervertebral disc contains intrinsic circadian clocks that are regulated by age and cytokines and linked to degeneration. I could have told you that 12 years ago but no one believed me. Who is laughing now? [http://ard.bmj.com/content/early/2016/08/02/annrheumdis-2016-209428.abstract](http://ard.bmj.com/content/early/2016/08/02/annrheumdis-2016-209428.abstract)

Ways to know your doctor doesn't have a clue about methylation:Â 

1\. He gives you FOLIC acid.Â 
2\. He gives you methyl folate, but without any B12.Â 
3\. He starts your dose of methylfolate at 20mg.Â 

4\. He doesn't control inflammation.Â 
5\. He gives you methyl folate in high doses when you're not having a bowel movement every day.Â 

Discuss.............

### Thor — 2012-12-13T12:07:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-67357>

.

There's even more it than thatÂ - there's only one Medical Doctor in the entire US that as a clue about this matter.Â  DR KENDAL STEWART, MD

He using transdermal formulas that by-pass the liver.Â  With gut issues you're lucky to get a 5% absorption rate from oral MTHF Tx

Â 

I've spoke with over 100 Doctors (MD & ND)Â  NONE of them had a clue about MTHFR SNP & proper treatment.Â  Choline is needed in larger doses, TMG is needed in larger doses and the list goes on.Â Â  MTHFR along with the SNP called SHMT affects Iron LevelsÂ - wonder why people have wacky Iron levels? Look at the role of SHMT & Iron - amazing.Â  Plus, too much Iron like I have along with most men causes germs to be MORE Virulent.

Â 

I could write a 50,000 page article on this matter alone.Â  If Doctors don't have a clue what & how genetics affect a patient - they can kill them.

I just had doctor try and put me on Niacin - Dear God !Â  Just one dose of Niacin is enough to send me into the hospital.Â  Niacin is a Methyl Sponge!Â Â  Niacin also plays a role in the COMT SNP - and it can dump lithium.Â Â  Then if a Doctor puts you on L-Dopa supplements - with COMT gene he better have BH4 onboard.Â 

Â 

Bottom line:Â  Trying to treatÂ MTHF patients is very tricky and I know first hand.Â  I'm having a horrible time finding a local doctor to help. ugh....

[http://youtu.be/J5m2jfiSnN4\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;J5m2jfiSnN4\]http://youtu.be/J5m2jfiSnN4\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;J5m2jfiSnN4)

### Thor — 2012-12-13T12:09:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-67358>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Â 

The conversion of 5,10-methylen-THF into 5-methyl-THF, which is catalysed by **[MTHFR](http://http://us.mg6.mail.yahoo.com/mthfr/allgemein.htm), is irreversible. The only way to makeÂ  further use of 5-methyl-THF and to maintain the folate cycle consists in theÂ  vitamin-B12-dependent remethylation of homocysteine to methionine (regeneratingÂ  THF). The methyl group transfer is therefore greatly dependent on 5-methyl-THFÂ  and the availability of **[vitamin-B12](http://http://us.mg6.mail.yahoo.com/B12/allgemein.htm). In humans, this is the only knownÂ  direct link of the metabolism of two vitamins; folic acid and vitamin-B12 bothÂ  need each other.
Â 
In cases of **[vitamin-B12 deficiency](http://http://us.mg6.mail.yahoo.com/B12/mangel.htm), it is possible that, inÂ  spite of sufficient availability of folates (and 5-methyl-THF), an intracellularÂ  deficiency of biologically active THF arises. This situation is called a â€˜folateÂ  trapâ€™** (or methyl group trap) because, on the one hand, the concentration ofÂ  5-methyl-THF continues to rise but, on the other hand, due to it being preventedÂ  from releasing methyl groups, a â€˜metabolic dead-end situationâ€™ develops, whichÂ  leads to the inevitable blockage of the methylation cycle. The co-factors forÂ  the C1-transfers decrease and replication as well as the cell division rate areÂ  reduced. Hence, the principal problem is the decreasing activity of methionineÂ  synthase under vitamin-B12 deficiency with secondary disorders affecting theÂ  folate metabolism and insufficient de-novo synthesis of purines and pyrimidines.Â  The deficiency in active folic acids first affects the quickly dividing andÂ  highly proliferating haematopoiesis cells in the bone marrow and can even leadÂ  to pancytopenia.
Â 
Clinically, there is now no difference between vitamin-B12 deficiency anaemiaÂ  and folic acid deficiency anaemia. If such an anaemia is treated withÂ  vitamin-B12, the blockage is immediately stopped and the [blood count](http://http://us.mg6.mail.yahoo.com/neo/blutbild.htm)**Â  quickly normalises. However, if the anaemia is exclusively treated with folicÂ  acid, it is simply converted to dihydrofolate and THF.
Â 
Long-term therapy using high doses of folic acid could therefore conceal theÂ  real cause i.e. pernicious (vitamin B12-deficiency) anaemia for a long time. TheÂ  serum folate continues to rise (congestion of non-regenerated 5-methyl-THF)Â  while the intracellular folate concentration (erythrocytes) drops. ThisÂ  situation interrupts the methylation cycle with numerous cell processes, amongÂ  them the synthesis of myelin, the nerve fibre lining, being blocked due to aÂ  deficiency of methyl groups. A long undetected (causal) [vitamin-B12-deficiency](http://http://us.mg6.mail.yahoo.com/B12/mangel.htm)** can therefore result inÂ  serious neurological damage.
Â 
Exclusive folic acid therapy can lead to damage to your healthÂ  or even death.

It turns out that if the MTHFR mutation is turned on in early childhood, that child has a high probability of developing an Autism spectrum disorder.Â  However, there are hundreds of thousands of adults with MTHFR, but they donâ€™t have autism.Â  Why not?Â  Itâ€™s all about the timing of theÂ  mutation being turned on.Â  If MTHFR is turned on later in life, such as during late adulthood, there is a high probability of a neuro-immune syndrome, such as Alzheimerâ€™s, Parkinsonâ€™s, ALS, etc.Â  In fact, itâ€™s quite common to see kids on the autism spectrum that have grandparents or older relatives with MS, Parkinsonâ€™s, etc.Â  If MTHFR is turned on during early childhood, one will have to work much harder to correct the issue than if it were turned on during adulthood.

http://functionmedicine.com/blog/?p=63

by Dr. Tim Jackson.Â Â  He trained with Dr. Stewart and will do phone consults.Â

â€Ž"Autism Night Before Christmas"
written by Cindy Waeltermann

\-----
Twas the Night Before Christmas

And all through the house
The creatures were stirring
Yes, even the mouse

We tried melatonin
And gave a hot bath
But the holiday jitters
They always distract

The children were finally
All nestled in bed
When nightmares of terror
Ran through my OWN head

Did I get the right gift
The right color
And style
Would there be a tantrum
Or even, maybe, a smile?

Our relatives come
But they donâ€™t understand
The pleasure he gets
Just from flapping his hands.

â€œHe needs discipline,â€ they say
â€œJust a well-needed smack,
You must learn to parentâ€¦â€
And on goes the attack

We smile and nod
Because we know deep inside
The argument is moot
Let them all take a side

We know what itâ€™s like
To live with the spectrum
The struggles and triumphs
Achievements, regressionsâ€¦

But what they donâ€™t know
And what they donâ€™t see
Is the joy that we feel
Over simplicity

He said â€œhelloâ€
He ate something green!
He told his first lie!
He did not cause a scene!

He peed on the potty
Who cares if heâ€™s ten,
He stopped saying the same thing
Again and again!

Others donâ€™t realize
Just how we can cope
How we bravely hang on
At the end of our rope

But what they donâ€™t see
Is the joy we canâ€™t hide
When our children with autism
Make the tiniest stride

We may look at others
Without the problems we face
With jealousy, hatred
Or even distaste,

But what they donâ€™t know
Nor sometimes do we
Is that children with autism
Bring simplicity.

We donâ€™t get excited
Over expensive things
We jump for joy
With the progress work brings

Children with autism
Try hard every day
That they make us proud
More than words can say.

They work even harder
Than you or I
To achieve something small
To reach a star in the sky

So to those who donâ€™t get it
Or canâ€™t get a clue
Take a walk in my shoes
And Iâ€™ll assure you

That even 10 minutes
Into the walk
Youâ€™ll look at me
With respect, even shock.

You will realize
What it is I go through
And the next time you judge
I can assure you

That you wonâ€™t say a thing
Youâ€™ll be quiet and learn,
Like the years that I did
When the tables were turnedâ€¦â€¦

For all of you who are interested in you FOLR SNPs (reduced folate) and GAD1, 2 mutations (glutamate/GABA issues) here you go.Â 
Methylation defects and what they can be found with.

FOLR3 rs7925545 +G/-A reduced folate carrier

FOLR3 rs7926875 +C/-A reduced folate carrier

FOLR3 rs7926987 +G/-C reduced folate carrier

GAD1 rs11542313 +C/-T heroin addiction

GAD1 rs12185692 +A/-C anxiety, depression, neuroticism

GAD1 rs2058725 +C/-T heroin and alcohol dependence/ oral cleft

GAD1 rs2241165 +C/-T panic disorder, neuoticism, bipolar disorder, heroin and alcohol dependence

GAD1 rs3749034 +A/-G schizophrenia, panic disorder, oral clefts, heroin dependence

GAD1 rs3762555 +C/-G panic disorder

GAD1 rs3791850 +A/-G anxiety, depression, neuroticism, alcohol and heroin addiction

GAD1 rs3791851 +C/-T anxiety, depression, neuroticism

GAD1 rs3791878 +T/-G schizophrenia, post traumatic seizures, alcohol and heroin dependence

GAD1 rs3828275 +T/-C post traumatic seizures, alcohol dependence

GAD1 rs769390 +C/-A decreased GABA levels by GAD1 and COMT

GAD1 rs769391 +A/-G post traumatic seizures

GAD1 rs769407 +C/-G anxiety, depression, neuroticism, heroin dependence

GAD2 rs1805398 +G/-T alcohol dependence

### freshveggies — 2012-12-24T14:29:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-68310>

Looks like I am homozygous for one of the Folr3 genes and also I should consider staying away from alcohol and heroin!Â  I have been working on the glutamate/gaba balance.Â 

From Yasko book:

If glutamate is not converted to GABA, the elevated levels of glutamate can cause
seizures and cell death, and elevated levels of glutamate also make even low doses of mercury more toxic.

Decreased GABA levels also increase anxiety, something that many of the chil-
dren, their parents, and even the practitioners working with them tend to experi-
ence. Dealing with autism creates tremendous stress.
Low GABA levels can also increase aggressive behavior, as well as decrease
social behavior. Low GABA leads to decreased eye contact and difficulties in
focusing. With some children, due to low levels of GABA, both eyes focus in-
ward toward the nose, while other children may experience horizontal or verti-
cal wavering of the eyes. Low GABA levels lead to decreased bowel function.
It may seem counterintuitive that a calming neurotransmitter helps stimulate
bowel activity, but GABA does indeed help the bowel to contract. In addition,
GABA release may help to reduce the triggering of transient lower esophageal
relaxations, decreasing gastroesophageal reflux (GERD). GABA is essential for
proper intestinal motility.

I am heterozygous for the C677T and the doctor that ran the MTHFR test recommended Thorne Research Methyl-Guard. I can only take it twice per day, AM and mid afternoon.Â  If I take it any later than that it will keep me awake pretty much all night.

Since then I've learned that I have other methylation mutations and am homozygous on quite a few (CBS A360A, COMT V158M, COMT H62H, BHMT 02, 04 + 08, and MTRR A66G). Waiting until after consult to add anything else to my supplement regimen. Think I will need to be very careful and start one new thing at a time to evaluate how it affects my body.

I've come this far.Â  A little more patience won't hurt me.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-30T10:33:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-68731>

High B12 levels wont hurt you.......but if your serum folate levels are also high it is a sign that your folate is not getting into you lipid soluble membranes and your methylation is slow.......it also means your methyl folate is garbage.........

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-17T17:04:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-71266>

Then on through the methylation cycle ...cleaning up each area as we go. It's really interesting and I think I will finally get to the bottom of my incredibly undermining issues with histamine. I'm very pleased with how Tim and Dr. Kruse's work goes together. The methylation issue kind of shoots you into a maze that needs some particular guidance and then you can come out and join the rest of the crowd heading towards optimal.

The GI test is key and the snps.

I am going to include scanned copies of all my labs from the last six months (have plenty!), a family history of both my maternal and paternal side, current meds & supplements, medical history, and Sterling's review of my 23andme SNPs. From my point of view, he should have as much background info as I can provide.

### RiverNeighbor — 2013-01-18T19:23:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-71499>

The spectracell presentation was good. I learned a lot. I really liked the case studies the Dr. presented. She practices and hour North of where I live, accepts my insurance, and I'm wondering if it might be worth taking my daughter to her. Hmmm... Maybe I'll call her office Monday to see if she works with kids. It has been so hard finding someone good locally for my kids to see.
You know... I think I might be starting to understand from all this why I get a rapid pulse, anxiety, and HIGH body temp in the morning for about 3-4 days before my period. I'm thinking Estrogen dominance rears it's ugly head as natural progesterone production drops off and I'm only left with what my BHRT gives me (testing again soon so I'll see how the BHRT is doing... my guess is that my ratio is still bad). That estrogen excess leads to more excitatory neurotransmitters (by further reducing MAO activity which I probably already have reduced by SNP) - hence the pulse and anxiety (and let me tell you how much of a disaster it is if I drink a cup of coffee in this state). I'm probably still magnesium deficient, probably not doing a good job of converting B6 to P5P (if i understand what's going on with my SNPS) so that's not clearing estrogen... or I'm just B6 deficient, probably not doing a great job of getting the estrogen cleared out via liver so some of that is probably recycling into my body, and all these extra fat cells I have are probably adding insult to injury by making extra estrogen. Hmm. I hope I'm actually starting to get this and am not just making up crazy theories

### RiverNeighbor — 2013-01-19T18:03:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-71667>

I can say looking into this has really helped with some of the things my kids had going on. We thought my son was just quiet - he talked but wasn't interested in just chatting. And he was getting stuck on food. He would pick one or two things and just totally lose it if I tried to feed him something else. Daughter was having a hard time with mood and tantrums and she just couldn't rest. Both would get hyperactive in an inappropriate way at times. I started using the neuroimmune stabilizer cream from Neurobiologix (a cream just seemed like such an easy way to get the vitamins into them and this is the only one I'm familiar with) on them the week before Christmas and we have seen massive changes. My son has become a chatterbox, is trying new foods (no octopus or MHS yet though, LOL), doesn't get into the hyperactive/"over-tired" mode. Daughter has stopped having multiple tantrums a day, is able to relax/rest and even take a nap, doesn't struggle for hour(s) to get to sleep at night, and she's been a social butterfly at the playground when she used to not really want to interact a lot with other kids.
We don't have any genetic info on them right now, but we're going to send their 23andme kits in today probably. I don't know if we'll consult with any of the Dr.s out there specializing in this though.

### fitness@home — 2013-01-22T20:25:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-72398>

Sorry this is kind of late...meant to post it earlier this week. There is a teleseminar tonight:

The Work of Dr. Amy Yasko Teleseminars

Tuesdays at 5:00pm PST, 6:00pm MST, 7:00pm CST and 8:00pm EST

The dial in number is 805-399-1000

The participant access code is 380752

Welcome everyone! My name is Tricia Stenzel and I will be moderating this series of teleconferences on "The Work of Dr. Amy Yasko". These teleconferences will be happening every Tuesday evening, 5:00pm PST, 6:00pm MST, 7:00pm CST and 8:00pm EST starting January 22, 2013.

As some of you know I used to be an Officer/Patient Advocate with a Lyme Disease Organization. I have had numerous people come to me about why they are not getting well from their various health issues. I wanted to help these people, and I also wanted to recruit the best Doctors in my opinion to help us understand what is going on with our bodies and explain things to us so that we can better heal. I am honored that Dr. Amy Yasko has asked Dr. Nancy Mullan and Mrs. Erin Griffin to help us with this. I would like to thank Dr. Yasko for her brilliant work and for asking us to present these teleconferences.

The topic for January 22, 2013 is

Your Single Most Important Biochemical Process: Methylation

Your body has a "traffic light" for its biochemical pathways. Dr. Nancy Mullan, with Mrs. Erin Griffin, discuss how it can work well, what can go wrong and what that means for you.

You can listen to the call and see the slides that Dr. Mullan will be explaining by logging on to

https://www.freeconferencing.com/meetings/437-519-566

Enter your email address and name to log in.

This one's for YOU!

We want your input. We would like you to tell us what you want to know. We want to know if you need something repeated. You can send your questions to patriciastenzel@gmail.com Your questions can be about anything. They do not have to be about the particular week's conference subject. The teleconference will last approximately one hour. There will be approximately one half hour of lecture and then I will have the opportunity to ask Dr. Mullan and Mrs. Griffin your questions. We will be adjusting what we are doing as we go along to make this teleconference the most satisfying experience for you. I urge all of you to listen to this important information.

The conference call in number is (805) 399-1000.

The participant access code is 380752

This is a US toll based phone number and depending on your long distance provider you may be charged for this call. You may have unlimited long distance. If you do not, long distance charges will apply.

All teleconferences will be recorded.

Call 805-399-1099 to hear a replay of the teleconference

This and other pertinent information will be posted on Dr. Mullan's Facebook page, my Facebook page and Dr. Yasko's Facebook page.

I will also be posting the following week's topic for discussion.

Mrs. Erin Griffin is the married mother of two sons recovering from Autism Spectrum Disorder. She has been the moderator of Dr. Yasko's CH3 Nutrigenomics Parent Discussion Forum since 2005. She is the go-to person for getting it right when doing Dr. Yasko's program. She is a tireless advocate for patients and is the leader and most prominent of the Cyber Moms, a group of knowledgeable, beautiful, generous, loving women who spend hours each day helping other mothers recover their children.

Author, Lecturer, Clinician, Nancy Mullan, MD, recovers patients using the methods of Dr. Amy Yasko. Educated at the University of Pennsylvania, Tufts University School of Medicine and the University of Chicago Hospitals, Dr. Mullan is best known for her genetics based clinical therapeutics. She has written a number of ground breaking papers with Dr. Yasko that can be found under Resources on her web site, www.NancyMullanMD.com . Their most recent paper, Aluminium Toxicity in Mitochondrial Disorders in Autism Spectrum Disorder is posted on their respective Facebook pages.

I am honored to be the moderator of this series of teleconferences and urge all of you to please submit questions or topics that you would like discussed to either myself at patriciastenzel@gmail.com or Dr. Nancy Mullan at nancymullanmd@aol.com because we want your input on what you would like to hear Dr. Mullan discuss. We are looking forward to a big turnout! You will not regret the time you spend with us!

Sincerely,

Tricia Stenzel

Riv, let me know what you think of that doc in Murrietta. I looked her up and saw a lot of people complain of the way she runs her front desk/appts. I hope you have better luck than they did.

There is another webinar tomorrow from MTHFR Support on Blog Talk Radio: http://www.mthfrsupport.com/blog-talk-radio.html

### Jack Kruse — 2017-10-30T13:56:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-access-your-doctors-knowledge-of-methylation.5152/post-226942>

Here is the 2017 update that is tied to today's November 2017 release:
Ways to know your doctor doesn't have a clue about methylation:

1\. He gives you FOLIC acid, and never mentions how solar exposure effects cells

2\. He gives you methyl folate, but without any B12, and never mentions how grounding increases light assimilation

3\. He starts your dose of methylfolate at 20mg, and never mentions how CT improves methylation because it depletes you of deuterium.

4\. He doesn't control inflammation, and never mentions that pH is log scale of proton action

5\. He gives you methyl folate in high doses when you're not having a bowel movement every day, and never mentions to you how the red light part of the sun control the motion of things like protons.

Discuss.............with this prediction I made 5 years ago to an old member:[http://www.iflscience.com/space/nasa-twin-study-shows-that-space-travel-alters-gene-expression/](http://www.iflscience.com/space/nasa-twin-study-shows-that-space-travel-alters-gene-expression/)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-experience-with-compounding-pharmacy-for-hcg.5156/post-67370>

I just started using HCG as a way of increasing testosterone- I am taking 500 units twice a week.Â  I bought a bottle from a local pharmacy ($150).Â  My issue is that I have to travel and its a pain to try to keep it cold.Â  I have heard compounding pharmacies are cheaper and my be able to provide in different size bottles?Â  anyone have any experience?

Â 

I found this one www.apsmeds.com

I have no idea if they are good or not

Â 

Thanks,

Ethan

### Becky Taylor — 2018-12-06T21:39:24+0200

(501) 315-7700 but they’re are saying the board of pharmacy won’t allow them to ship out of state. They are in Arkansas.

### JanSz — 2018-12-06T22:04:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-experience-with-compounding-pharmacy-for-hcg.5156/post-257428>

I make 450iu shots
Out of 10000iu I get 22 doses, each of 10 units volume.
Use bacteriostatic water
Preload 22 syringes and keep them in the freezer.
When I travel I take with me # of syringes and keep them without any refrigeration attempts.

# Thread: VENTING
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Awainer1 — 2012-12-13T20:14:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67412>

I went to have a followup today for a physical and labs that I got done with a doctor I found through my insurance network. He tried to put me on a statin and made me sign a release form saying I wouldnt take any medicine. I paid out of pocket for labs in august and had a consult with Dr Kruse. I asked the doc him to check my lipids, vit d, dhea, and thyroid. He told me the thyroid panel came with dhea and then I got it back. No dhea, no vitamin D. I was really trying to work on my Free T3 by doing epipaleo and Dr Kruse said that I could try using cytomel. My FT3 went down and my TSH went slightly up since August doing epipaleo. My cholesterol went from 361 total to 386 total. The doc also wouldn't prescribe my cytomel because he said i was in the acceptable range which was at 2.6 on a scale of 2.3 - 4.2. I have another consult upcoming with Dr Kruse so I will talk to him about where to go next but its REALLY frustrating when your trying to help yourself and the medical profession is not on your side. Guess I need to lower my expectations. Hopefully its just another step in my process towards optimal. Thanks for listening to me vent!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-12-13T20:26:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67413>

I went through five docs before I found one who would even say I had a problem with my thyroid.....all the while my labs clearing showing Hashimotos antibodies out of control.Â  Take heart and find another doc.Â  The one I have now is only OK on some things just not aggressive enough for what I want.Â  I'm looking too!

### Awainer1 — 2012-12-13T20:39:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67414>

Thanks for your support.Â  I guess I shouldnt over react to one doc especially knowing what I was getting into.Â  It was just really frustrating my dad has heart disease and the first thiing he asked me was "do you look like your dad" and I was like no and he said that I had the same health as him and then he asked me what it was going to take to do something implying that I needed to be on a statin before I had a heart attack.Â  I'm 27 years old.Â  I guess at this point I should be aware of what dogma does to people ibut it still sucks to have it thrown in your face. At least i got my lipid and thyroid panel done for free instead of paying for it out of pocket. They also made me come back to get my results cause they told me it was a two part physical and then charged me 20 dollars for the second visit.Â  When I told them I didnt want to come back in the first place they told me that they have to go over such an abnormality or they could be liable. But when I told them before they even got my results they said I had to come back for them.Â  Sorry.Â  I'm just really discouraged but I will stay positive as I still have come a really long way.

### Destiny — 2012-12-13T20:55:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67416>

I know how upsetting this is, so it is good to vent. Then you can start looking for another doctor. I hope you will be lucky and find a good one quick!!

### Awainer1 — 2012-12-13T21:15:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67422>

Thanks I'm really glad to get support here cause I was feeling really helpless.Â  I dont know where else to look for a doc casue there aren't any in close proximity to me on the paleo physicians network.Â  I guess I will just have to keep working on it.Â  I have thought about going the naturopathic route but they are really expensive and dont take insurance and I dont want to pay a lot of money if I dont know that they support a "paleo" lifestyle.Â  I will keep you updated on how it goes.

### endless — 2012-12-13T21:22:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67425>

I don't know if you have compounding pharmacies near you, but they are a good place to ask....I went to my local one and asked which doctors prescribed the most natural hormones, etc.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-13T21:25:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67426>

> **Awainer1 said**
> I went to have a followup today for a physical and labs that I got done with a doctor I found through my insurance network. He tried to put me on a statin and made me sign a release form saying I wouldnt take any medicine. I paid out of pocket for labs in august and had a consult with Dr Kruse. I asked the doc him to check my lipids, vit d, dhea, and thyroid. He told me the thyroid panel came with dhea and then I got it back. No dhea, no vitamin D. I was really trying to work on my Free T3 by doing epipaleo and Dr Kruse said that I could try using cytomel. My FT3 went down and my TSH went slightly up since August doing epipaleo. My cholesterol went from 361 total to 386 total. The doc also wouldn't prescribe my cytomel because he said i was in the acceptable range which was at 2.6 on a scale of 2.3 - 4.2. I have another consult upcoming with Dr Kruse so I will talk to him about where to go next but its REALLY frustrating when your trying to help yourself and the medical profession is not on your side. Guess I need to lower my expectations. Hopefully its just another step in my process towards optimal. Thanks for listening to me vent!

Awainer,Â 

Â 

Do not get frustrated. Â Become stronger in your resolve. Â The difficulty lies not so much in developing new ideas as in escaping from old ones. Â Understand the cesspool that your doc lives in......but you do not have to accept mediocre because you know better. Â It often takes a crisis to get people moving past themselves and that includes a doctor. Â I know because that is what moved me to change. When we begin to feel fear and we fear loss we are capable of extraordinary things. Â Right now you don't trust.........stop. Â And trust. Â You have the power. Â Don't believe everything you thinkâ€¦â€¦..from our personal ruins rises our transformation. Â Our thoughts do not have expiration dates unless we delete them from our consciousness. Your current thoughts in this thread might just alter your DNA in the future. Â Ponder that reality. Â 

Â 

Iâ€™m still a doctor, and still a man of science every bit as much as I was before I had my experience. But on a deep level Iâ€™m very different from the person I was before, because Iâ€™ve caught a glimpse of this emerging picture of reality. And you can believe me when I tell you that it will be worth every bit of the work it will take us, and those who come after us, to get it right.Â  Biology always makes sense if we see it from its perspective and not our own.

Â 

Embrace the panic and chaos of illness, because of its mere presence, you inspire your own creativity to find your pathway back to health and subsequently soothe your mind. How did I come to this conclusion? Â  One of the lessons found in human history is that even the deepest crises can be moments of extreme opportunity. They bring us ideas from the fringe of our mind into the mainstream of our daily consciousness.

Â 

Every mistake we experience in our life and in our own health creates an opportunity for a healthy recovery.Â  We cant just live our life, we must create it and live that result. Â AWainer...........never forget this lesson. Â You have the antidote to this doctors advice in you.......do not let his opinion subjugate what your mind has cultivated. Â You are a starfish and it is time for you to make it happen for you..........DO NOT SETTLE when you know better...............

### Shijin13 — 2012-12-13T21:38:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/venting.5160/post-67431>

It took me 10 yrs to find someone willing to work w/me outside the box. You are your best advocate. Interview your docs and tell them you're looking for a partner in achieving optimal health. Keep pushing for what you need no doctor will advocate for you. Only you can. In sales the day it takes 100 no's to get a yes. Every no makes you a better salesman/woman for your health. Own your Health. Arm yourself with knowledge. The doc your looking for will think you're a breath of fresh air compared to their refular patients

# Thread: Colder in a warmer environment?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colder-in-a-warmer-environment.5163/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mike NYC — 2012-12-13T23:12:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colder-in-a-warmer-environment.5163/post-67458>

Hi everyone!Â  This is actually my first post here, but I tend to read a lot more than I post.... especially since forums are blocked in my office during the day.

Anyway, a strange thing happened last week.Â  I was in Arizona where it was about 75-80 during the day and probably 55-60 at night.Â  In NYC, where it has been warmer than usual with highs in the 40s and lows in the 30s, I still have not worn a jacket and never feel cold when outside.Â 

However, one night when I was there I was shivering while talking to a friend in a parking lot at In & Out burger after we ate, though I did not feel uncomfortably cold I did not feel warm like I usually do here.Â  I also was noticing the temperature change in the evenings more than I would have expected.Â  Maybe it has something to do with the lower humidity but it was strange.

I definitely ate less fat there just because I was eating out a lot, but I did manage to get in at least a decent sized order of shrimp once a day.Â  For CT I mostly just go outside and put ice packs on my neck and femoral artery, which I find tremendously relaxing, but I have not gotten into the full ice baths yet.... small tubs in nyc apartments....

Â 

Thanks for any input!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-14T08:00:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/colder-in-a-warmer-environment.5163/post-67476>

reward is all about pleasure.......see this is why a guy like Guyenet does not get the brain......he thinks reward is a one way street. Â It is not. Â What is rewarding in one environment to us is not rewarding in another because it depends upon the perspective of the person presently. Â Epigenetics and Einsteins observer bias are one and the same.......in a way

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-12-13T23:24:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vo2-max.5164/post-67460>

Today I had a VO2 max test done at my gym.Â  http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/VO2\_max

My test was on a treadmill and lasted about 10 minutes. The most interesting part of the results were the fat vs. carb burning.

Wasn't sure what to expect since I stopped doing all cardio at the gym for 3 months, and just resumed cardio in late Sept.Â  Currently I do cardio about twice a week for 20 minutes, and consider it moderate effort since I can talk comfortably at any point.

Results summary:

Â Â Â Â Â  heart rate (HR) range 136 to 143Â  --->>Â  fat utilized (low of 69%, high of 92%) ... carbs utilized (low of 8%, high of 21%)

Â Â Â Â Â  HR range 146 to 150 -->>Â  fat utilized (between 52% and 69%) .... carbs utilized (between 31% and 48%)

Â Â Â Â Â  HR range 151 to 155 -->>Â  fat utilized (between 6% and 52%) .... carbs utilized (between 48% and 94%)

Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  (the test uses the math of fat plus carbs utilized = 100%)

Thus, my heart rate had to reach the low 150's before I used more carbs than fat as energy. Good results according to my trainer. He said most people that he tests have less than 50% fat utilization as the highest number when the test begins measuring fat vs. carb use.

I plan to retest in a month to see the results of a n-1 experiment I am doing.

A quick review of the blog revealed that Dr. K mentioned 2 key items that improve VO2 Max results:Â  1) CT and 2) epi-paleo diet.Â  Since I haven't done much CT lately, I believe that my consistent epi-paleo diet for the last 6 months helped me become a fat burner. Stopping the chronic cardio I did prior to my 1st consult w/ Dr. Kruse made it much easier to reduce my carb intake. Â

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-14T08:17:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vo2-max.5164/post-67478>

Many more need to test themselves in this manner.......CT is very effective and its effect is additive to the result.

### Inger — 2012-12-19T15:25:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vo2-max.5164/post-67973>

IDK if this had anything to do with VO2.. but I have been feeling so funny about my breathing last weeks. At work I have to run upstairs to the cabinet serving groups up to 30 people partying and eating menues with starters, main course and dessert. So it could get exhausting... but not for me. I have found, I breath so slow and deeply when situations becomes demanding and stressing.. I intuitively breath very deep down in my abdomen.. and it feels nice and calming and it gives lots of additional power. It calms me and does amazing things! Makes me focused. Very focused. I do not get tired. And it is not conscious..?! I just realize I do it. It feels nice. I often realize I breath so slowly. Very slowly. It feels nice though so I am not worried one bit..lol

I wonder if it has to do with anything above.

It is too hot at work but I have it cold at home.. and I am eating Epi Paleo for sure. Lots and lots of seafoods and seaweed too. Every single day.

.

Of course anyone on this forum already knows this /wp-includes/images/smilies/icon\_smile.gif Â Â  but....Â Â  An excellent report on using food as medicine - crazy right lolÂ Â  I've used this to share with Doctors in hopes of explaining the Epi-Paleo diet & how effective it is for health.Â 

Â 

**Starving Cancer: Ketogenic Diet a Key to Recovery

Â 

[http://www.cbn.com/cbnnews/healthscience/2012/December/Starving-Cancer-Ketogenic-Diet-a-Key-to-Recovery/](http:///www.cbn.com/cbnnews/healthscience/2012/December/Starving-Cancer-Ketogenic-Diet-a-Key-to-Recovery/")**

Â 

Many people are frustrated with today's cancer treatments. They are expensive, painful and often just don't work.

### Destiny — 2012-12-16T15:07:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-report-on-ketogenic-diet-cancer.5188/post-67651>

Thanks, Thor!

Â 

This is very interesting book that is expanding on your post. Unfortunately, it is out of print right now.

Â 

http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/0470584920/ref=as\_li\_ss\_tl?ie=UTF8&camp=1789&creative=390957&creativeASIN=0470584920&linkCode=as2&tag=chrisbeatcanc-20

Â 

Reviews:

Â 

Dr. Seyfried provides compelling evidence that cancer is a metabolic disease (NOT a genetic disease), and this has major implications for the treatment and prevention of cancer.

According to Otto Warburg's theory of cancer, mitochondrial dysfunction is the origin of cancer. Dr. Seyfried has amassed extensive evidence to support Warburg's theory and advances the idea that cancer arises from defects in energy metabolism (mitochondrial dysfunction), and that this metabolic dysfunction triggers genomic instability by activating oncogenes, inactivating tumor suppressor genes, etc. The author does an incredible job at convincing the reader that healthy mitochondria are the ultimate tumor suppressor.

It's clear that the strategy to treat cancer as a genetic disease is not working, but this fuels the pharmaceutical industry. Enormous amounts of money are spent on large cancer genome projects, but this has not advanced our understanding or treatment of cancer. The information from these genome projects has actually created more confusion amongst cancer researchers, and this is very clear if one reads the literature. On the other hand, when cancer is viewed as a metabolic disease the strategies to treat and prevent cancer become incredibly simplistic and economical. For example, animals studies, case reports and anecdotal evidence demonstrate that metabolic therapies that lower blood glucose and elevate ketones will shrink tumors, extend lifespan and in some cases cause complete remission. This strategy is effective because cancer cells are fueled by glucose and lack the ability to derive energy from ketones (due to mitochondrial defects). Metabolic therapies exploit the mitochondrial defects associated with cancer by targeting glucose metabolism and elevating ketones. These therapies are simple and include "therapeutic fasting", calorie restricted ketogenic diets and relatively cheap and safe drugs that target cancer-specific metabolic pathways. The information in this book is valuable to patients and supported by extensive references. This book is technical (in parts), but the author has the ability to describe complicated processes with elegant simplicity.

I read this book immediately after reading "The Secret History of the War on Cancer" and "The Emperor of All Maladies". These are excellent books, but I found Dr. Seyfried's book to be more informative and empowering because it gives the reader clear evidence for the cause of cancer and highly effective strategies to prevent and treat the disease.

I would highly recommend this book to all cancer researchers, oncologists and layman interested in understanding the origin, management and treatment of cancer as a metabolic disease. This book will also be an extremely valuable resource for patients diagnosed with cancer.
Dominic D'Agostino, PhD, University of South Florida

\------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

I am a board-certified medical oncologist with 30 years experience in caring for cancer patients and another 20 years of research in cancer medicine dating back to 1963. Seyfried's "Cancer as a Metabolic Disease" is the most significant book I have read in my 50 years in this field. It should be required reading of all cancer specialists, physicians in general, scientific researchers in the field of cancer and for medical students. I cannot overstate what a valuable contribution Thomas Seyfried has made in writing this masterpiece.
Stephen B. Strum, MD, FACP
Medical Oncologist, Member of ASCO (American Society of Clinical Oncology) since 1975

In the video it was great to see the lady reporter had her facts straight and hit the highlights of the ketogenic diet.Â  Most likely alot of old and sick people watch that channel and got to see the report. I was tickled when she told Pat there WAS one side effect...Â Â  WEIGHT LOSS!

No mention though that a ketogenic diet should work for nearly all other diseases, too.Â Â 

I've started telling certain people around work they can pay for the right foods now or they will be paying for even more expensive health care down the road. I saw that saying elsewhere in the forums and sort of adopted it.

At work there was an employee appreciation day recently.Â  They fed the employees tacos, chips, cookies, and candy.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-12-19T14:59:27+0200

>
> "some tumor cells also ferment glutamine and possibly other amino acids in the presence of elevated glucose and O2. Glucose and glutamine interact synergistically to drive tumor cell fermentation."

My remark about thiamin was related to a University of Ohio paper which indicates tumors can use a thiamin dependant pathway to create more ribose for DNA/RNA synthesis. Sorry for any confusion.

### nonchalant — 2013-01-08T20:58:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-report-on-ketogenic-diet-cancer.5188/post-69976>

> **ATL\_Paleo said**
>
> Ray's link did not work ... this one shouldÂ 
> Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â  http://cassiopaea.org/forum/index.php?topic=29102.0
>
> Very interesting read.
> I was fascinated by this part:
>
>
> Experimental evidence shows that "Normal mitochondria suppress respiratory dysfunction and tumorigenicity, whereas abnormal mitochondria cannot suppress respiratory dysfunction or tumorigenicity."
>
> (a) Normal cells beget normal cells.
>
> (b) Tumor cells beget tumor cells.
>
> (c) Delivery of a tumor cell nucleus into a normal cell cytoplasm begets normal cells despite the persistence of tumor-associated genomic abnormalities.
>
> (d) Delivery of a normal cell nucleus into a tumor cell cytoplasm begets tumor cells or dead cells, but not normal cells.
>
> The results show that nuclear genomic defects alone cannot cause tumors and that normal mitochondria can suppress tumorigenesis.

All these researchers investigating genetic causes of cancer are just chasing red herrings. Profitable red herrings though they may be.
Take care of your mitochondria, people!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-02-24T22:06:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-report-on-ketogenic-diet-cancer.5188/post-156496>

Light is controlled by universal physical laws and biology. Every cancer on this planet has been linked to ubiquination defects. All ubiquination defects are associated with altered melatonin and sulfated Vitamin D levels. Everyone knows that lightcontrols the SCN........few realize the science that dictates light actions is QED and not biology. Therefore, you beliefs about food/biology link to cancer are not material nor accurate. Harsh.......but totally true. Most people know I support ketosis in cancer. Here is another shocker: ketosis in cancer won't work well, unless your SCN is working faster than your organ or tissue clocks. Why? the SCN has to run faster to control cell signaling. That is the point your missing. Regarding observational and non controlled studies about optical photonics and cancer. They will be USELESS, and are only a delay tactic. Why? The science that dictates this are universal laws, not biologic ones. They are not subject to a RCT or your beliefs. They are already firmly established by deep experimental proofs in physics. Don't forget all life is made up from atoms. Absence of evidence suitable to you is not really material in this arena. Moreover, it is not absence of effect when you begin to understand what a dielectric blocker does to water optically..........

### Inger — 2012-12-17T17:32:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunman-was-vegan.5197/post-67743>

Too sad. An organic vegan because of moral/believes. It sound so healthy and good doesn't it?
I can tell it touches something in me too, as I have a mental ill younger brother that I whitnessed getting SO MUCH BETTER with an epi paloe lfestyle last summer, but as he got talked around from some vegan friends it went south and fast. Now he is hospitalized and very bad off.

I could cry out loud when I see, again and again, how the ignorance and numbness of folks around contribute to the suffering in our communities. The unwillingness to recognize that what you eat matters HUGE!
My own parents believe all what I tell them, and do some stuff too, but they still are, in practice, totally ignorant of the fact that what we eat forms who we will be. They plain do not get it, like serious. They do not see, how darn serious it is.

Sometimes I get such a strong feeling.. people are SLEEPING...?!
WTH.. they are like half dead, really. I see them around me all the time. Sometimes in the train, I look around at peoples faces when I have my dark glasses on at night. It is almost like in a horror movie... I mean, there do are sunshines in between, but the majority.. it is something very wrong.
And then, people are suprized when stuff like this happends? I am not one bit. I am suprized, they are not happening more often!

### caroline — 2012-12-17T17:54:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunman-was-vegan.5197/post-67745>

I was asking my Doctor about this yesterday. I am the only one in his practice with all these questions - so we are learning together. He said - mostly patient's eyes just glaze over and they want a quick fix. How can you not want to know about things that can and will make your life better? It is exciting for him \[his words\] and he says we are learning together. It is kinda scary that in some things I know as much as he does and some things we just have to leave for Dr.K. to sort out. I guess - that in itself is remarkable - he wants to learn from JK as well.
I think that is what makes us all unique and alike here - even tho we are all so, so different. Most of us have been fighting for years for more information to make our life better and we never gave up and may have taken lots of detours......but now JK has given us the tools and we just have to do the work. I am having the time of my life!

### SeaHorse — 2012-12-18T09:40:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunman-was-vegan.5197/post-67801>

That's really a good description DD and Ingar....people being asleep or the waking dead. I find it most difficult to be around large groups of people, the lack of health and vitality seems overwhelming then. It is important to maintain really clear boundaries so that we don't get sucked into the vortex of despair that is all around us....and we have to be vigilent to see the light and be the light because that is always there too...we just don't feel it when we're clobbered with the reality of our fellow humans.

Â Without shame, I've chosen to not engage in all the news about the shooting. I learned the basic facts and have not listened to anything since. I need to be able to feel my feelings rather than be fed a whole lot of sensationalized language and visuals. If we can bring the experience back to ourselves and feel our own responses we stay true to what it means for us. That leaves us open to reaching out in a way that isn't shaped by a kind of "group think" mass process. Grief and sorrow are powerful and healing emotions and I don't fear them....despair isn't, so when I start to despair or feel depressed (lots of experience with that) I know I have to review what's going on with me and change something up.Â

If we can bring the experience back to ourselves and feel our own responses we stay true to what it means for us. That leaves us open to reaching out in a way that isn't shaped by a kind of "group think" mass process. Grief and sorrow are powerful and healing emotions and I don't fear them....despair isn't, so when I start to despair or feel depressed (lots of experience with that) I know I have to review what's going on with me and change something up.Â 

I loved what you said Seahorse ...Large groups of people Â .. even/mostly ones I love ....really worry me. Â  Christmas is really worrying me - it is so hard to be around all these very unhealthy people and you always seem to be defending your position even tho the results are so obvious. Â

### Adria — 2012-12-19T10:55:46+0200

Â They arrive in the morning, some are huffing from the walk from the parking lot to the elevators. Â Then they go after the bakery, some shooting insulin to eat it. Â  Â Yesterday they had the annual holiday luncheon. Â I'm always the only one that doesn't attend. Â I know behind my back.. they call me anti-social, not a team player. Â

### PrimalPam — 2012-12-21T10:52:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunman-was-vegan.5197/post-68117>

The first moment I heard about this tragedy, I wondered what the gunman's diet was like. I just KNEW it had to be something like this. Not enough DHA, not enough MCT, too many carbs. Myelin sheaths not optimal. I wonder if he was breastfed or given soy formula, like so many of us? We'll probably never know, unless his father talks about it, but not getting mother's colostrum is the first thought.

I immediately came to this forum because I wanted to re-read what Dr. K. has been telling us for a year about how autism is formed by our lack of proper epi-paleo nutrition, not the vaccines. He's said it over and over. I bet he feels sometimes like he's screaming his head off in a cave. Read Dave Asprey's story; fascinating.

People keep asking WHY. But it's the wrong WHY. They want to know WHY he did the shooting. They should be asking WHY was his brain so messed up in the first place? Go back to his birth and follow his nutritional patterns. Oh, and let's not forget the thousands of hours he played video games. I'd bet my next paycheck he stayed up till all hours of the night. He lived in the basement, so very little sunlight. I could go on.

My heart just breaks for all these poor little schoolchildren.

And I, too, feel sometimes elitist when I walk through a mall and look at all the sad-looking, bloating, inflamed people. Until I remember my own d\*mn self a very short time ago.

### Greg rx — 2013-01-17T18:44:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gunman-was-vegan.5197/post-71288>

I love blaming the veggie diet but I have some observations that NOBODY is talking about.
All/most of these gunmen have no fathers who taut them the real use of firearms and respect for their power.
All/most of these gunmen are already being treated with SSRIs witch are famous for causing suicide in teens. Thanks big pharma
These shootings are a suicidal acts so prevention after they have made the decision is futile.
Having played these shootem up video games with my kids, I know that weapon selection is a big part of the game. I predict that if and when assault weapons are denied, the next option is going to be a bolt action sniper rifle from a high perch with good cover. Mark my words. Assault weapons are just for fun, a hand gun or riot shotgun would be more efficient.

# Thread: I found heaven....!!!

**Subforum:** The Cave

### Inger — 2012-12-18T08:48:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-found-heaven.5207/post-67799>

I am dancing, dancing.... just so full of joy I could explode.. HUH!!!! A new word is lately singing in my ears.

Serendipity.

It happends all the time now!!!!

WTH? I am living in my parents house for half a year now (I am allowed to live here for free in an own appartement upstairs when I take care of the animals, help a bit in the house and do snow work and in summer garden work -wich I all love!), that has been cursed by so many of my sibling, saying it kills them, the athmosphere.. and here am I living, and THRIVING...? I could not last time I lived here years ago. Now, everything have changed?!

Â 

This makes me even more then 100% sure, we are 100% responsible for our own happiness, and **everyone** can find heaven. Maybe it is already around?

Got to get some more fish and cold and dark in..lol. oh wait.. this is already too good! I just cannot take more for now! I love this!!!

Now out to do some snow work! { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded.
