# Thread: My blood glucose level actually went DOWN after eating a meal...without any diabetes medication!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-blood-glucose-level-actually-went-down-after-eating-a-meal-without-any-diabetes-medication.5279/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2012-12-28T18:54:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-blood-glucose-level-actually-went-down-after-eating-a-meal-without-any-diabetes-medication.5279/post-68624>

Talk about motivation!

Last week, for the first time since I was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes over 10 years ago, my resting A1c levels were within "normal" range, which made me feel elated.

This afternoon, 15 minutes before my lunch, my blood glucose level was 5.9 mmol/L or 106.3 mg/dL.

For lunch I had some salmon cooked with a bit of fresh lemon juice, and some kale cooked in coconut oil, and some spices. Along with 2 squares of 90% dark chocolate, 2 squares of 99% dark chocolate, both covered with a thin layer of coconut manna. I also took 2 chewable Vitamin C tablets. Plus two thin slices of medium cheddar cheese, about 1/16" inch thick by 3/4" x 2".

Anyway, 2 hours after I finished, I took my blood glucose reading again, and it was 5.7 mmol/L, or 102.7 mg/dL!!Â 

It actually dropped after I ate! Not a significant amount, but still a drop! And that was without any diabetes medication!

Talk about motivation and incentive.

Yes I realize that the cheese isn't the best thing to have, but I still have some in my fridge, and I hate to waste food, or throw it away. Besides, I don't think a few pieces now and then is going to kill me.

On a side note, at times Firefox doesn't allow me to respond again in the LeptinRx forum after I post, so if I don't respond to any replies, it's not because I am being rude, but for some reason my javascript becomes null and void, and won't allow me to. So I apologize in advance if that happens.

I wonder too, how much your CT might already be playing into this? Together they're a powerful, powerful tool.

so I can one day serve others like I really want, bold and daring, full of amazing power, wisdom and patience, from a 300 000 foot wiev!

We blow way too many things way out of proportion...IMHO, and can be way oversensitive about certain things. I think worrying over ever little miniscule detail all the time will actually cause far more stress on a person than whatever POSSIBLE damage the piddly ass thing they're worrying about could POSSIBLY do. I just don't want to have to worry about every single, little aspect of every single thing I do, 24/7. I want to relax ONCE in a while and just enjoy life. LOL.

### seanb4@hotmail.co.uk — 2012-12-29T12:38:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-fasting.5284/post-68664>

Hi guys,

I have hadÂ gastrointestinal issues that appeared upon eating and wouldn't be fixed by all kinds of alternative treatment (Gaps,Â FodmapsÂ  betaine hcl, and many manyÂ more). The main symptoms where heavy bloating, constipation, and fatigue, plus tons of other smaller symptoms.

I started a fast boxing day and I am currently 96hrs in without eating or drinking anything (inc water). I plan on doing around 3-4 weeks until true hunger returns. I will start drinking water when I get thirsty for it.

In this time I have experienced die off in the form of nausea, weakness, tiredness, headache. My bloating is gone and I have experienced no real hunger or thirst in this time. I think my body does not want me to eat as it is spending all itsÂ energyÂ onÂ repairingÂ me.

So was wandering if anyone has any science to back up/explainÂ what'sÂ going on or if anyone wants to chip in with their experience.

Thanks, Sean.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-03T04:54:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/long-fasting.5284/post-69102>

Hi seanb4. I have nothing scientific to say about fasting. I can only tell about my own experience. I once fasted for 10 weeks on nothing but water and tea.
After a while I was never hungry again, but it did do damage. What I remember was, that my uterus shrank to the size of a walnut. I had an examination after the fast and thats how I know. For 10 years after that I could not get pregnant. (not that I wanted to, as I was quite young at the time of the fast (20) but I never used birth control either. My metabolism came down and even little food made me gain. I fasted a few more times in my life which resulted in more and more gain until I finally weighed 290 pounds.
So in the end I can say that the fasting did not resolve anything, but contributed to the already existing problems.
I think that a sensible epi-paleo diet will resolve your issues effectively.

My metabolism came down and even little food made me gain. I fasted a few more times in my life which resulted in more and more gain until I finally weighed 290 pounds.
> So in the end I can say that the fasting did not resolve anything, but contributed to the already existing problems.
> I think that a sensible epi-paleo diet will resolve your issues effectively.

That's interesting and I am sorry to hear it. Was your reasons for fasting wieght loss related?

I have been eating epi-paleo for a while, done cold thermogenesis and many many other things over the past year and nothing touches my stomach/diestion issues. So far fasting seems to be doing something....

I will definitely not go to 10 weeks, 4 weeks will probably be my max!

### Charlotte — 2013-01-03T08:00:10+0200

No reason to be sorry, was just as well. Was not ready to have children anyway as I had other issues, mainly with myself. Now I do have two brilliant sons who love their Mom and we are a closely knit family.
Yes, all I did in that respect was always for weight loss. I do not have any serious issues besides the weight and what comes with it and what i acquired trying to lose weight and the bad food choices I had in the past. I am as healthy as they come. Haven't had a cold in 20 years, never ill, can eat what I want (except what I found out regarding food intolerance like eggs, grains), but even eating these foods did not give me any problems except not being able to lose weight and I am physically very strong.

# Thread: TV Watching  Epi-Paleo Rx
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-watching-epi-paleo-rx.5287/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-12-29T16:49:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-watching-epi-paleo-rx.5287/post-68698>

.

This is something I've been researching and looking into; how TV watching started the fall of our health.Â  It's clear cancer wasn't around 100 years ago until we became more modernized.Â Â 

Watching TV actually turns your brain from functional into a near death stateÂ called Alpha wave pattern.Â  Alpha waves are present at different stages of the wake-sleep cycle. The most widely-researched is during the relaxed mental state, where the subject is at rest with eyes closed, but is not tired or asleep. This alpha activity is centered in the occipital lobe.Â 

Would be rather interesting to see how watching TV could effect living an Epi-paleo life & destroying your Sleep/Wake Cycle.Â  Which ties into so many other areas of our health.Â  Remember autophgy?Â  Just a few minutes of TV watching could alter this important process.Â 

Many people may not realize that when they turn on the television in their home, what they see as a constant flow of images is actually flickering. Although we do not see this consciously, the repetitive pattern of flickering images creates a state that is similar to hypnosis in the television viewer. Studies by researcher Herbert Krugman have shown that within 30 seconds of television viewing, brain waves switch from predominantly beta waves, indicating alert and conscious attention, to predominantly alpha waves, indicating an unfocused, receptive lack of attention. The brain's left hemisphere, which processes information logically and analytically, tunes out while the person is watching TV, while the right hemisphere of the brain, which processes information emotionally and non-critically, is allowed to function without hindrance. Due to this phenomenon, television transmits information, which is not actively thought about at the time of exposure, much like hypnosis. When viewing television, we do not consciously rationalize the information resonating within our unconscious depths at the time of transmission and the viewer becomes more open and suggestible.

Â 

Â 

Â [http://youtu.be/gGibMrc3G74\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;gGibMrc3G74\]http://youtu.be/gGibMrc3G74\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;gGibMrc3G74)

### JoeBranca — 2012-12-31T13:51:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-watching-epi-paleo-rx.5287/post-68834>

It was Neil Postman's book that convinced me to turn off the TV years ago. It is potentially damaging on several levels, as all aspects of the human experience are repackaged into a seamless entertainment stream.

However I am and always will be an advocate of film; done right it is the newest long form narrative that is the main commodity market of human idea sharing and powerful, cultural myth making in our era. It encompasses a language of semiotics that extends back through communicative arts down the ages, and in interesting ways is pushing forward in collaborating with the cognitive edges of the viewer.

Is anyone else finding this as well? What is everyone doing??? I am on my own so I guess I will have to figure out how to enjoy my own company more! any ideas???

### prAna303 — 2013-01-01T16:31:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-watching-epi-paleo-rx.5287/post-68932>

Is anyone else finding this as well? What is everyone doing??? I am on my own so I guess I will have to figure out how to enjoy my own company more! any ideas???

I did this journey about ten years ago. What i did was, throw the TV out, find a passion for motion. I spend most of my time in a element, like water, woods, mountains and so on. When you connect with the natural world time and space become so different. So my idea is find your natural passion and life become so very exciting. Making art of everything you do is what really matters...

### ashryn — 2013-01-05T09:37:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tv-watching-epi-paleo-rx.5287/post-69409>

> **Thor said**
>
> .
> I prefer the 1930's radio shows - ahhhh the good old days.Â  Do kids today even know what a radio is? LoLÂ Â  Dick Tracy, Dragnet ect....
>
> [http://www.dumb.com/oldtimeradio/listen/8956/Detective\_Dragnet/Dragnet/490721\_City\_Hall\_Bombing.html](http://http://www.dumb.com/oldtimeradio/listen/8956/Detective_Dragnet/Dragnet/490721_City_Hall_Bombing.html)

We used to print up scripts from those old radio shows and sit around the lounge with friends and take parts to play.. I wish I had recorded them, it was so fun.

Interesting post Thor..
I wonder if the way we watch tv these days might prevent us from slipping into that hypnosis state.. I always have my iPad on my lap and am continually looking things up as ideas occur to me when watching tv.
One of the best things (with 20/20 hindsight) that happened to me as a kid was that our TV broke and mum and dad decided not to replace it. For 4 years we only watched TV at friends houses, and when we were home and wanted entertainment we used to listen to the audio of the videotaped tv shows we had collected..

As a consequence, I am not a fan of tv and have never paid money for one... Though people keep giving them to us! We only watch tv we have downloaded specifically. Not having pay tv helps too.

It does make me a bit Internet challenged though, because so much info is on YouTube and I hate learning from videos.. I always look for written information and its getting harder to find..

# Thread: Will Cold Thermogenesis cause Muscle Enzymes CK levels to rise?

### furface — 2012-12-29T18:38:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-cold-thermogenesis-cause-muscle-enzymes-ck-levels-to-rise.5289/post-68706>

Just wondering if Cold Thermogenesis...especially when first starting out, will cause Muscle Enzymes CK levels to rise?

I started my CTÂ  therapy about 3 weeks ago, and last Friday I had a Muscle Enzymes CK blood test done (along with several other tests), and my result was 725 U/L from a Reference Range of <165 U/L.

I repeated the test last night, and my result was 305 U/L from the same reference range.

So my CK level had dropped over 400 points in one week, but when I saw a Dr. today, he said he wasn't really concerned, and that a fear of a possible heart attack wasn't a major concern of his, and said that the CT could have been an issue, but I'd like other viewpoints, esp Dr. Kruse if possible.

I see my Diabetes/Endo Dr. on Jan. 10th, and he's the one who ordered the CK test in the first place, so hopefully he'll have more input as well.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-30T10:24:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-cold-thermogenesis-cause-muscle-enzymes-ck-levels-to-rise.5289/post-68727>

# Thread: Your Bones Regulate Metabolism  Leptin Levels - Interesting Research
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-bones-regulate-metabolism-leptin-levels-interesting-research.5294/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-12-30T19:26:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-bones-regulate-metabolism-leptin-levels-interesting-research.5294/post-68781>

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

.

Ran across this today in my reading.Â  I've never heard of this before - our bones regulate our metabolism & leptin levels.Â 

Research is now pointing to our bones as an endocrine gland.Â  Boy - just when I thought I was getting a grasp on how everything works,

this comes along and blows my socks off.Â  Bones as an endocrine gland? But more than that - a gland that regulates leptin levels?Â  My head is

still trying to take this in.Â  This really drives home the point of taking mega doses of Vitamin D and K2.Â 

Don't chew me out if this has been posted before - sometimes I might miss previous entries.Â  Just passing this alongÂ 

\----------------------------------------

The bones used to be thought of as inanimate structures within the body. However, new research performed by Columbia University (Nancy Heim, Columbia University Medical Center, August 2007)Â have found that the skeleton plays an important role in the communication processes involved in energy metabolism.

An important signaling protein, osteocalcin, is produced by the bone building osteoblast cells. Osteocalcin is known to increase bone density by helping to funnel calcium and other minerals into the bone matrix. Other authors compare the function of osteocalcin to a vacuum that sucks calcium from the bloodstream and into the bone matrix.

New research has also now indicated that osteocalcin directs the pancreas to produce more insulin and induces fat cells to release the hormone adiponectin. Adiponectin acts upon cells to increase insulin receptor activity and therefore improves insulin sensitivity.

Researchers were surprised to discover that blood sugar metabolism is regulated by a protein released from bone tissue. Osteocalcin is the first hormone ever found that synergistically regulates insulin secretion and insulin sensitivity. Osteocalcin was also shown to boost the pancreatic beta-cells which produce insulin as well as reducing overall fat stores.

"The discovery that our bones are responsible for regulating blood sugar in ways that were not known before completely changes our understanding of the function of the skeleton and uncovers a crucial aspect of energy metabolism,â€ said Gerard Karsenty, M.D., Ph.D., chair of the department of Genetics and Development at Columbia University Medical Center, Paul Marks Professor in the Basic Sciences, and senior author of the paper. â€œThese results uncover an important aspect of endocrinology that was unappreciated until now.â€

Karsenty and his colleagues had previously shown that leptin, a hormone released by fat cells, acts upon and ultimately controls bone mass. They reasoned that bones must in turn communicate with fat, so they searched bone-forming cells for molecules that could potentially send signals back to fat cells. The researchers found that osteocalcin, a protein made only by bone-forming cells (osteoblasts), was not a mere structural protein, but rather a hormone with totally unanticipated and crucial functions.Â  Now, bones are tied to Leptin!!!!!!!!Â 

Â Â This discovery showed for the first time that one hormone has a synergistic function in regulating insulin & leptin......Â 

Osteocalcin activity is dependent upon adequate levels of vitamin D3 and K2 and regular exercise. The vast majority of society is deficient in both of these crucial fat-soluble vitamins. This appears to be the link between vitamin D3 deficiency and diabetes. Osteocalcin will not adequately activate unless vitamin D3 levels are sufficient and K2 is present.

PS>>>>>Â Â  Guess what Diabetic Medications Do To Bone Health?!!?!!Â  yep - destroys bone health making diabetes only get worse!

### hellojtm — 2012-12-31T23:34:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-bones-regulate-metabolism-leptin-levels-interesting-research.5294/post-68883>

happy that this brought my attention to the osteo blog which i hadn't read yet.

### Thor — 2013-01-01T18:43:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/your-bones-regulate-metabolism-leptin-levels-interesting-research.5294/post-68943>

.
Ok - so it took some digging to find the section on Bones and Insulin in the blog. However, to go even one step further here's something that wasn't talked about in the blog.

Since roughly 60% of the population has one mutation on the MTHFR pathway... That means most people will need increased B-vitamins. More so if you're positive on both MTHFR genes - which means you're a slow methylator. Guess what... B-vitamins play a large role in bone health along with Vitamin D & Vitamin K. B-vitamins are the keys to make D & K work at the bone level. Everything ties back into LR and IR. How about that

Here's the article:

B Vitamin for Bone Health

Previously, studies have investigated the role of B vitamins to maintain bone health. Z Dai, from National University of Singapore (Singapore), and colleagues analyzed data from 63,257 men and women, ages 45 to 74 years, enrolled in the Singapore Chinese Health Study. The researchers found that those women with the highest quartile intake of Vitamin B6 (0.78-1.76 mg/1,000 kcal/day) had a 22 % reduction in hip fracture risk, as compared to women in the lowest quartile intake (0.37-0.61 mg/1,000 kcal/day). B-vitamins are being studied as to how they play a role in bone health.

Osteoporos Int. 2012 Dec 13

### Jack Kruse — 2012-12-31T11:10:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/happy-new-year-to-everyone.5296/post-68806>

Well I have decided to head to the Gulf.........Last night I ate a massive amount of oysters and stayed up way too late discussing my lifestyle with many old friends who were rather shocked at the new me. Â Today so far I have gone out and bought good homemade salami and Nawlins' coffee and did some weak ass version of CT. Â Kruse magic is simple........tap your inner creative genius in 2013 and become your own master. Â 

In 2013 consider becoming a great leader. Great leaders do not see what is, they see what can be. So if you want to be creative, look at the world around you with different eyes. Look through the lens of wanting to add value to others. What needs do you see around you? Our world needs "your" creativity. Can you tap it this New Year? Creativity begins in the mind, is expressed in the spoken word and out worked in action. It can be the result of the convergence of two or more different thoughts intersecting uniquely. Creativity is not just about artistic talents and performance; it is a reflection of your life. By design we each are creatively special and different on purpose, to serve purpose. In 2013, let us cultivate and value our differences, our uniqueness and learn to "blend" in with the uniqueness of one another. That's what I call the intelligence of humanity......The world is waiting for you to liberate your creativity.

Creativity is the expression of unique intelligence.

### Destiny — 2012-12-31T18:42:22+0200

!

### freshveggies — 2012-12-31T18:59:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/happy-new-year-to-everyone.5296/post-68869>

I am with you on not messing up the circadian rhythm.Â  My son opted out of an all night lock-in at the church because to not messing up his sleep and possible getting sick from lack of sleep.Â  I asked him if he was ready to feel real tired and not do anything all the next day and he said--Mom, I knew you would say that.Â

### fitness@home — 2012-12-31T19:03:08+0200

### maggie — 2012-12-31T19:44:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/happy-new-year-to-everyone.5296/post-68873>

I have always had a nagging melancholy during the holiday season and couldn't wait for it to be over. This year I just feel a quiet happiness. Every day. Family get-together with my son and his SO went beautifully--we spent a week together very pleasantly and they asked me if I would be willing to move to Northern California to be closer to them.Â  Huh?Â  Could it be epi-paleo and all that good DHA, iodine, etc?Â  It sure is a change--but without fireworks or effort.Â  The CT and the raw oysters--now _**that**_ requires effort-- but if this is the payoff, I'm in.

I wish everyone on the forum a very happy New Year and my thanks for your generous sharing throughout 2012.Â

### seanb4@hotmail.co.uk — 2012-12-31T08:39:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gnawing-sensation-early-morning.5298/post-68804>

Hi,

Sorry for creating so many topics. Just trying to sponge up as much info as possible!

Ever since taking too many NSAIDs over a year ago I have have a gnawing/hunger sensation right at myÂ solar plexus early morning (4-6 am). At first this was very strong and painful but after some ulcer treatment it reduced massively.

However it has still been present ever since and seems to depend on how my diet is. If I am eating good non irritating food (GAPs, epi-paleo, etc) it is very light and I only notice it if I happen to wake up at that time. If I eat say a lot of dairy the gnawing sensation is a lot stronger in the morning. Also still notice this when fasting all though it is fairly light.

Any idea what this is?

All comments areÂ appreciated!

# Thread: Do Fish on Mercury Contain Earth? Plus other weird thoughts-Kathylu's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 63
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathylu — 2012-12-31T12:27:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-68812>

I have been toying with the thought of journaling for some time now. Well, actually not, since I hate writing. More accurately I have been thinking I should journal. So here goes! No promises about how often I will post though.

Just listened to the December webinar...very good! I think it ties together many of the threads JK has been weaving for us for some time. Unfortunately, I am still waiting for him to say "Just kidding! Go ahead and eat whatever you want. You've done the leptin reset now and magically all your problems are solved! Then spend some time relaxing in the hot tub and don't forget to watch TV until you go to bed (after your late night snack of course)!" Okay...since that's not gonna happen I wanted to mention what I thought was the most important thing Jack said during the webinar. The thing that gets us to the real payoff. It was his discussion of "mitochondrial leakiness" and it's effect on cellular respiratory efficiency. Now it's kinda fun to understand the details and I would love a discussion of what affects electron transport and leads to the generation of ROS (reactive oxygen species), but it really doesn't matter as long as you get the take-away message. It was (paraphrasing) "The more efficient fat-burner you become, the fewer carbohydrates and the more fat you eat over the course of your life, the longer you will live and the healthier you will be." Kinda sums things up, doesn't it?

I want to be here in 50 years (I'll be 103 then) to see the public and medicine finally catch up to what _we know now_.

### kathylu — 2013-01-04T01:04:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-69257>

Hi Anna. Protein can certainly be turned into glucose, but it is a relatively "expensive" metabolic process (gluconeogenesis), so it occurs at a low but fairly steady rate. Protein can be used as fuel through deamination during protein metabolism, where some protein is converted to glucose in a process called gluconeogenesis, the formation of glucose from non-carbohydrate sources.

The basic difference between protein and carbohydrate is that while carbohydrates are made out of simple sugars (carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen), protein is made from amino acids (carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and sufur). The nitrogen is a basic component of the protein's amino acids and accounts for 13 to 20% of the total mass.

The first step in protein metabolism is to break it into its constituent amino acids. These are absorbed into the blood stream through the gut. Of course, it is not a complete process as many smaller proteins and protein fragments can be absorbed through the gut lining just as well as the basic amino acids (even larger proteins can be absorbed if your gut wall is very "leaky"). Many of these amino acids are used in the denovo synthesis of other proteins, some act in transport systems (for minerals for example), in signal transduction in hormonal pathways and as regulators of gene expression (mTOR is one example).

The second step is to break down the amino acids into their constituent parts (catabolism). This removes the nitrogen or amino group from the amino acids. The process is called deamination.Deamination breaks the amino group down into ammonia and what is termed the carbon skeleton. Ammonia is converted to urea, filtered through the kidneys, and excreted in urine. The carbon skeleton--which is composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen--can then by used either for protein synthesis, energy production (ATP), or converted to glucose by gluconeogenesis.

Most authorities believe that the amount of protein converted to glucose is quite small, except under conditions of intense exercise or metabolic starvation. Under these conditions amino acids produce the major source of glucose for blood sugar maintenance. Although the body (and brain) can run very well on ketones produced from fat metabolism, it does require some glucose. There is some disagreement about how much...the figure I see most often is anywhere from 75-150 grams per day. However, it is not necessary to eat carbs to provide glucose, since all the body needs it can produce from protein.
So the answer is yes, protein can be used for fuel as well as repair and maintenance. However, once you have keto-adapted (become a fat burner), fat is the preferred fuel source.
One good reason to try to keep your protein level moderate is that some amino acids are quite insulinogenic (the bcaa's and arginine especially). If you produce a lot of insulin from excess protein, you stop burning fat just as you would if you ate too many carbs. So the higher the percentage of your calories come from fat (as long as carbs are very low), the faster your weight loss should be. Of course, I am completely ignoring the contribution of hormonal regulation here, so YMMV.

You don't need to be a scientist to understand this stuff...you just need to own a biochemistry text. Jack says this all the time...lol.

### kathylu — 2013-01-04T10:20:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-69284>

Ashryn, I think you are spot on. In fact when a sugar burner starts a low carb diet, many get the "low carb flu"...fatigue, nausea, headache, muscle aches. It takes some time (about 7 to 14 days) for the genes that code for enzymes that burn fat to upregulate to make fat burning efficient. Jack can say more about this than I can, but I know one of the problems that accompanies leptin and insulin resistance is "metabolic inflexibilty". Think of fuel use as a fuel "gauge", with one end being "sugar" and the other end being "fat". If you are leptin and insulin sensitive you can easily switch back and forth between the two fuels, depending on what is available in your food sources seasonally. If you are resistant the gauge can get "stuck" on sugar burning. This is pretty universal in type II diabetics...they are almost obligate sugar-burners!

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-05T23:23:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-69539>

Kathy, I love your scientific mind and your willingness to explain things! Thanks.

I'm not sure you want me/us using your journal to ask you questions, but if you have a moment I'd love your thoughts on this related question. You stated, "So the higher the percentage of your calories come from fat (as long as carbs are very low), the faster your weight loss should be." Are there risks to high fat (assuming good sources of fat) and moderate/high seasonal carbs at the same time? I ask because my children, who seem metabolically optimal, eat high fat (lots of butter, animal fat, fish/seafood) but I also don't restrict their seasonal carbs. They eat the fruit that is fresh in season (currently things like berries, cherries, and plums, as well as citrus... soon to be grapes on our grapevine, too), dark chocolate, sweet potatoes and the occasional white potato, too. And of course they eat protein too, mostly from eggs and fish and meat, though they also eat some dairy. Is there a way that the high fat can cause them problems? Should it be restricted to winter when the carbs are less? Or with the seasonal eating and the use of solely high-quality, healthy fats, is it still OK? (This is something I've always wondered about... as a mum, I can worry about almost anything!)

Thanks, Lauren

I think 2013 might be a great year...for all of us!

Anne.

### kathylu — 2013-01-07T02:32:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-69711>

> **Anna K. said
>
>
> > kathylu said
>
> **
>
> > If you produce a lot of insulin from excess protein, you stop burning fat just as you would if you ate too many carbs. So the higher the percentage of your calories come from fat (as long as carbs are very low), the faster your weight loss should be. Of course, I am completely ignoring the contribution of hormonal regulation here, so YMMV.
>
> Kathylu, thank you so much for your detailed explanation. One thing is not clear to me from your statement above. As you said, if insuline spikes from excess protein you stop burning fat. But why would this happen? If you ate too many carbs, your body will obviously burn it for fuel first and not use fat. But if you ate too much protein, just like eating too much fat, they both will releases energy for ATP production during Catabolism http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catabolism. besides, there is no evidence that eating excess protein speeds up gluconeogenesis. So why would protein stop your body from burning fat any more then dietary fat would?
> Perhaps excess protein interferes with weigh loss via some other pathway? like appetite control or some hormonal regulation pathways, etc not because it stops you from burning fat?

Insulin inhibits lipolysis and stimulates lipogenesis. If you have a lot of insulin floating around, you can't burn fat efficiently because insulin blocks hormone sensitive lipase (no cleavage of fatty acid esters can occur). Insulin increases lipogenesis by increasing the concentration of malonyl-CoA, a substrate required for fatty acid synthesis. Too much insulin signals a "fed" state to the brain and fat storage occurs easily. Doesn't matter if the insulin is "spiked" by protein or carbs, the result is the same...you store calories as fat.

### kathylu — 2013-01-07T02:56:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-69712>

> **KiwiLauren said**
>
> Kathy, I love your scientific mind and your willingness to explain things! Thanks.
> I'm not sure you want me/us using your journal to ask you questions, but if you have a moment I'd love your thoughts on this related question. You stated, "So the higher the percentage of your calories come from fat (as long as carbs are very low), the faster your weight loss should be." Are there risks to high fat (assuming good sources of fat) and moderate/high seasonal carbs at the same time? I ask because my children, who seem metabolically optimal, eat high fat (lots of butter, animal fat, fish/seafood) but I also don't restrict their seasonal carbs. They eat the fruit that is fresh in season (currently things like berries, cherries, and plums, as well as citrus... soon to be grapes on our grapevine, too), dark chocolate, sweet potatoes and the occasional white potato, too. And of course they eat protein too, mostly from eggs and fish and meat, though they also eat some dairy. Is there a way that the high fat can cause them problems? Should it be restricted to winter when the carbs are less? Or with the seasonal eating and the use of solely high-quality, healthy fats, is it still OK? (This is something I've always wondered about... as a mum, I can worry about almost anything!)
> Thanks, Lauren

Hi Lauren. It might be really interesting for you to calculate the macronutrient ratios of your kids' diet. I'm just wondering if they are really eating more fat than most people do? It sounds like your kids are doing great. I personally think that good fats are the most benign source of calories. So, if they are getting 15 or 20% protein, lowish carbs and a lot of good fat, I would think they would be supremely healthy! What I try to do with my son is to teach him good habits. I don't tell him not to eat things he likes (as a treat), I just try to get him to connect how he feels with what he eats (of course, he's 5 so I have a lot of control at this point). In general, it's not a good idea to eat a LOT of carbs with a lot of fat, but you know that already. I think an occasional indulgence is fine in an otherwise "primal" kid. Just my opinion.

### kathylu — 2013-01-27T04:51:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-72835>

Ok...I never promised to post frequently! Here's the next topic.

I'm feeling pretty bummed out recently, some of it due to all this EMF stuff. Not to be technical or anything! Mostly, I am wishing all of this had come to light earlier. Why, you ask? Because a smart meter was installed on my house on 11/20/12! I'm usually no dummy, but they certainly got me here. I got a letter saying my electric meter would be upgraded and what a good thing it would be...lots of info for me to be able to be more "green", reduce my energy consumption, help keep electric costs low, etc. Sounds great, right? Well, now that I'm looking into it, it seems smart meters are spawn of the devil. I'm wondering if it is responsible for my daily mild headaches, my worsened asthma and poorer sleep (less than 7 hours), which coincidentally seemed to start about 2 months ago.

I immediately wrote to the electric company demanding that they remove the meter due to my electro-sensitivity and received an automated "not a chance, please feel free to jump off the nearest bridge" reply! Ok, on to plan B...finding out how to shield my house from the sucker. There is a way. Of course, it involves a satellite dish, lots of foil, carbon fiber cloth and a grounding cable...lol. WTF?

Assuming my husband would think I was crazy, I explained what should be done and why. Sometimes the universe simply provides what one needs! My husband didn't laugh, he said "If you think we need to shield the meter, I believe you and by the way, I never threw out the satellite dish we took down when we switched to FIOS (fiber optic cable)...

So, the inside of my garage wall is now covered in tin foil, has a satellite dish affixed to it (with the reflective side out of course) and is grounded via copper wire to the electrical panel. Thank goodness no one can see this jury rigged set-up! It's only been up for 2 days, but I actually think I am feeling better. No headache this morning and last night I slept for...9 hours!

Now, because I am a moral person, I am going to tell my neighbor that we are sending all the EMF in his direction...

### kathylu — 2013-01-27T15:14:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-72852>

> [Coriander said:](/goto/post?id=72846)
>

>

Coriander, immediately go and check you electric company's and state's policy on smart meter installation! I found all this out after the fact, but I believe most places have a "deferral option". Most do not allow you to "opt-out", but as long as you keep your deferral request (by letter or email) active, they cannot install the meter.

Good luck! Do this now and you may be able to avoid the problem.

### kathylu — 2013-02-01T17:04:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-73663>

Ok everyone, here's the link to the satellite dish shield idea:
[http://www.electricsense.com/2431/smart-meter-shielding-tips/](http://www.electricsense.com/2431/smart-meter-shielding-tips/)

It was easy to do (the meter is on the outside wall of my garage), just wrap the dish in tin foil, affix it to the wall that has the smart meter on the other side and run a grounding wire to your electrical panel. You can increase the shielding area by covering a portion of the wall with tin foil as well. Home Depot/ Lowe's sell steel panels that might look a little less "home made" and would probably work too. I can't give you numbers on the drop in RF (my emf meter hasn't arrived yet), but I do feel better. This is all subjective however. Please note that some people say shielding is not as effective as you think because the RF pulse is transmitted along your house wiring, emitting "dirty electricity". Someone else needs to comment on this...I don't know much about this subject. Best plan is to have the thing removed. I'm working on it!

### kathylu — 2013-02-03T18:19:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-73958>

Slipping, slipping, slipping. Had a bad day yesterday. Actually, the day was pretty enjoyable, but I didn't follow JK rules. No cold bath (I just can't talk myself into it after 2 pm or so). We decided to go out to dinner and I chose a Thai restaurant, thinking I would easily be able to get allowable food there. I ordered Tom Kha Gai (chicken and coconut milk soup) which would have been perfect if it had not been sweet! I ate it anyway. I ordered Deep Sea, which is a curry with shrimp, scallops, mussels, clams, squid and green onion. Perfect, except I ate a 1/3 cup of white rice with it (and the sauce tasted a bit sweet too). My DH and 5 yr old DS shared an order of pad thai (noodles with peanut sauce), since they occasionally still eat grains. I ate a few forkfuls of it too. Went home and had 3, count them, 3 glasses of red wine too! I enjoyed every bite, slurp and sip. Watched a movie on tv that ended at midnight (blue blockers on of course, but still). Slept well, up at 8 this morning...I don't think I deserve it, but I'll take it!

Not sure what caused this temporary insanity, but I'm back to the program this morning. Sardines and 3 tbsp of fish roe, bacon and wilted greens for breakfast, coffee with HWC. I'm stuffed. I guess it has something to do with not wanting to ask the restaurant to accommodate my dietary needs. I'll have to work on that. Time for the cold bath....

### endless — 2013-02-03T18:59:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-73963>

Don't sweat it...sounds pretty benign as far as cheating goes! It's not like you were stuffing pizza and cheesecake in your face. I am exactly the same with CT...2pm seems to be my cutoff. I actually TRIED to have red wine last night, but my body did not want. I was in the mood to cheat, so ate some strawberry basil sorbet I made months ago that was in the freezer.

If they bring me the rice or potatoes, I will eat some of them. I know that about myself. Sometimes I still 'forget' to say not to bring them.....

### kathylu — 2013-02-04T18:05:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-74084>

Wow...30 minutes at 48 degrees yesterday. Small admission here...I have been very inconsistent with CT since last summer...meaning I have done it only occasionally. I decided to get back into regular CT and have been having to cold adapt all over again. My body temp was 98.1 when I got in and 98.2 when I got out...pretty good. However, my temp dropped later and I felt cold and occasionally shivery for 3 hours afterward! So, cold adaptation is still in my future, I guess. Trying again today. Also, I'm only submerging from my ribcage down, belly is covered by cold water, but not my upper 1/3. I don't know what it is, but I just can't seem to get used to full submersion. I still think you get the benefit though. Your body's cold receptors activate when skin temp is less than 65 degrees, so I should still be encouraging the conversion of WAT to BAT.
I had evil detox reactions the first time I tried this, headaches, flu symptoms, muscle aches and ravenous hunger. This time...no detox symptoms (well, none yet anyway). Wonder why? I'm sure my fat cells are filled with crappy toxins...maybe my liver detoxification is better at this point.
This morning my FBG was 112, a little higher than normal for me (it's usually around 105), probably the wine I drank last night. I am still taking cycloset and metformin. My HbA1c has dropped from 5.7% to 5.4%, but my FBG is still high. Cycloset still gives me mild nausea in the morning...I have to eat HAB quickly or I won't be able to eat due to nausea. That happened this morning, my timing was off due to issues with the child, so I managed to eat a bowl of bone broth with chicken, coconut oil and greens in it, but then the nausea hit and I couldn't take another bite. Probably only 15 g protein this morning. I expect I will have to eat lunch today. The only thing I can manage is to drink green tea until this feeling passes.

### kathylu — 2013-02-05T06:24:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-74250>

Ok! Thirty minutes at 47 degrees today. Only the first 5 minutes was uncomfortable, so I tried submerging a bit more today. I'm still going to need to work on this, I don't like it! I warmed up after abiut 90 minutes, so better than yesterday. I really think CT every day is the ticket...the easiest way to get used to it.
Heard an amazing story on NPR today. A doctor is exploding the myth that the baby boomer generation is healthier than the previous generation. His conclusion? That baby boomers say they are healthier, but the numbers don't support the idea. There is significantly more T2D, heart disease, cancer than before. And diseases formerly associated with old age like Parkinson's and Alzheimers are showing up in people in their 40's and 50's. He also concluded that there may be a "perceptual problem", stating that maybe baby boomer's expectations are too high. "Maybe they expect to be healthier than it is possible for their age cohort to be".

Jeebus.

### kathylu — 2013-02-06T17:11:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-74439>

Here's a topic I would like some input on. My previous experience with CT was miserable. I did it practically everyday (from 2/12 to 8/12) and had terrible detox reactions. Towards the end it was easier, but I still got mild headaches, muscle aches etc. I was doing it basically on faith. I did lose 15 lbs, nothing to write home about but since nothing else seemed to be touching the year long stall I was in, I stuck with it. I stopped during the late summer, telling myself I would take a break until the weather was colder. Well, the break was longer than I intended, I just seriously re-started this past week. I'm not making excuses, I just got sick and tired of lugging all that ice upstairs to the bathtub (60 to 80 lbs worth) to cool the 78 degree water down to 50. It's easier now that the water comes out of the tap at 50 degrees, I just add 4 one quart frozen bottles and it takes the temp to about 47.

The interesting thing I have noticed is that I am having no detox reactions at all...outside of a mild muscle soreness that dissipates in a few hours. Why, why, why? I expected this to be just as hard as when I previously was doing CT. I do not believe that I completely cleaned my body of stored toxins. What's different? Could it be all the work I have doing to balance my hormones? Correct the upside down Pg/E2 ratio? If I could tell people that it is possible to CT without the absolute misery I went through initially, I might be able to convince a few people to try it. Thoughts?

### kathylu — 2013-03-07T17:26:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-79475>

I just wanted to tell you this morning's funny moment. I always invite the family to come with me to "ground" in the morning. I step outside barefoot, look for the sun and walk on our slate path, then grass in a large circle. Occasionally my 5 year old son and hubby will come along, but they both think I'm a little crazy (if they only knew!). This morning, I mentioned it would be better to ground naked, to get the photoelectric effect as well. I was really joking, since the path I use is in the front of my house with no privacy from the rest of the neighborhood. Anyway, I notice that the house is too quiet, so I look outside and there is my 5 year old, buck naked, in 30 degree F weather, running the path in circles shouting "I'm grounding!" at the top of his lungs...

I did have to collect him...even though I could barely stop laughing!

### kathylu — 2017-04-28T03:55:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-213017>

Hi folks! It's been forever since I posted. I do read, try to keep up with Jack's blogs, but life events have kept me away (many of them good!). Anyway, here goes.
I'll pose my question first. **Should I have a colonoscopy?** I have really been going back and forth on this and could use some opinions. Some background first...I'm 56, have been following the epi-paleo diet for the last 5 years, and have seen spectacular improvements in my health! I lost 50 pounds, got my HbA1c below 5.5, cured my horrible, horrible arthritis, felt like I made great strides in addressing my sleep issues, leaky gut issues, asthma and seasonal allergies have all but disappeared, hormones in better balance, no more depression and probably other things I am forgetting to mention. Things were going swimmingly...then December 2016 happened.

I was CT'ing in a surface stream near my home in rural Maryland and I managed to swallow a mouthful of stream water, details don't matter, just an accident. I didn't think anything further about it, but exactly 8 days later I thought I had contracted cholera! Terrible, explosive diarrhea up to 20 times per day, mild fever, dehydration, mild nausea, terrible bloating and gas. No blood in my stool though...it seemed to fit the profile of an infection with giardia lamblia. Greasy, odiferous stools that float (steatorrhea). After reading that giardia usually resolves itself in a few weeks, I waited a bit (3 weeks). No real improvement, so I went to the doctor seeking antibiotics (I know, I caved, but I was sooo damn miserable!). Did stool tests, which showed nothing. However, giardia is difficult to detect, a minimum of 3 stool tests are recommended before a deciding on a true negative result. Did a 7 day course of Flagyl (metroniadazole) and by day 2 the symptoms had improved markedly. By day 7, I thought "This is going to work!". Things much better (not quite normal, but acceptable). Continued to improve, but **8 days after finishing the antibiotics, the same symptoms come roaring back!** Funny, exactly 8 days. Anyway, I call my GP who reluctantly agrees to give me another course of metronidazole, 10 days this time. She says if this doesn't do it, it's a colonoscopy for me. I object, saying "Etiology! If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, then it's likely a goddamn duck...". She says I fit the profile for colon cancer...over 50, still overweight, sudden change in bowel habits, etc. I say I'll consider it.

More in a few minutes...family calling!

### kathylu — 2017-04-28T04:29:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-fish-on-mercury-contain-earth-plus-other-weird-thoughts-kathylus-journal.5299/post-213018>

So, I complete the second course of antibiotics, with great improvement again. Still not totally normal, but ok. No blood detected in my stool samples. Blood tests ok, no anemia, but my hs-CRP has shot up to 3.83! Less than 1.0 whenever I have had it tested in the last few years. So I think "that squares with an infection...but damn, it could also indicate cancer!". So, off to the GI specialist I go. I repeat my story, she does a physical exam. No pain, just a little all over discomfort (dyspepsia). I say "Do I need a colonoscopy?" She says "Maybe...up to you." She thinks I have post infection gut dysfunction, which apparently happens often after an infectious gastroenteritis. She states that if I had colon cancer, I would likely have not seen any improvement on the antibiotic. Also, with this level of symptoms, that I would probably be experiencing severe pain. I say "How long will this last?", she replies "It could be years...so sorry." What do you recommend? Bland diet, use loperamide if the diarrhea is bad, zantac for GERD, if it improves my symptoms. No dairy, no wheat (which I was doing before all this started anyway). Other than that, I'm on my own.

You can tell from my writer's bias thus far that I think this was an infection. However, I have this little nagging doubt at the back of my mind that says "What if it **is** colon cancer?" There's the rub. Do I submit to a potentially dangerous, rather invasive test to get a definitive answer? Or, do I "go with my gut?" Pun intended.

I have returned to Jack's "Leaky Gut Prescription". Epi- paleo, bone broth, NAC, L-glutamine, colostrum, high dose fish oil, sunlight, etc. I am seeing some improvement, but I am still way behind where I was. I am **stunned** at how fast my health could deteriorate. Aside from all the gut problems, my joint pain has flared up (somewhat migratory), asthma is back, a little eczema too. It looks like leaky gut has returned...

So what do you think? Go with the obvious, or eliminate the potentially possible? Help. please.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Responding to Dr. Kruse's reply to my thread about having a problem at times eating 50 gr of protein
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responding-to-dr-kruses-reply-to-my-thread-about-having-a-problem-at-times-eating-50-gr-of-protein.5307/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2013-01-01T14:05:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responding-to-dr-kruses-reply-to-my-thread-about-having-a-problem-at-times-eating-50-gr-of-protein.5307/post-68916>

For some reason Firefox keeps voiding my javascript on the Leptin Rx forum, and won't allow me to reply after starting a thread. So I'm replying in a new thread. My apologies. I've checked my settings, and it still won't help.

> Dr. Kruse said:
>
> Here is something for you all to read regarding proteinâ€¦â€¦..and why Rosedale needs to be questioned.
>
> The Role Of Dietary Protein In Optimizing Muscle Mass, Function And Health Outcomes In Older Individuals
>
> Wolfe RR. The role of dietary protein in optimizing muscle mass, function and health outcomes in older individuals. Br J Nutr 2012;108 Suppl 2:S88-93. http://journals.cambridge.org/â€¦..id=8724365
>
> The determination of whether increased dietary protein can positively affect health outcomes is hindered by the absence of prospective, randomized trials directly addressing this issue in which all pertinent variables are controlled. Consequently, we can only address the question deductively by considering the support for the rationale underlying the notion of a beneficial effect of increased dietary protein intake.
>
> With regard to health outcomes, we have focused on older individuals. Muscle mass and function are progressively lost with aging, so that by the age of 60 many individuals have reached a threshold where function begins to be affected. An association between reduced muscle mass and strength and unfavourable health outcomes is more likely to be revealed in individuals who have significant decrements in muscle mass and strength.
>
> In this article support for the rationale underlying the notion of a beneficial effect of increased dietary protein intake is considered. Dietary protein intake, and the resulting increased availability of plasma amino acids, stimulates muscle protein synthesis. If all other variables are controlled, increased muscle protein synthesis leads to improved muscle mass, strength and function over time. Increased muscle mass, strength and function are related to improved health outcomes in older individuals.
>
> Since adverse effects of reasonable increases in protein intake above the recommended dietary allowance (RDA) of 0.8 g protein/kg/day have not been reported, it is reasonable to conclude that the optimal protein intake for an older individual is greater than the RDA.

This is very interesting, and I agree. I have no problem with the amount of protein required, or suggested by Dr. Kruse in the Leptin Rx BAB, no problem whatsoever. I LOVE protein!! My issue is actually physically being able to eat that much at times. Some days I have no problem eating 50, 60, even 70 grams for my BAB, but other days, I feel as if I'm going to throw up after 35 grams or so. I just can't seem to ingest it on those days. I don't care how beneficial it may be, or even how necessary it is that I do it, on some days I just can't seem to physically manage to eat a minimum of 50 grams of protein for my BAB. I want to, but I just can't without forcing the food down my throat...which causes me to feel like crap throughout the day.

And no, I am not ready to eat raw meat to make it "easier", so please don't go there...AGAIN!

Maybe I need to add more fat to my BAB, but then that still doesn't help me meet the minimum requirement of 50 gr of protein on those days.

Oh well, I'll keep doing my best to cram it down my throat.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-01T14:14:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responding-to-dr-kruses-reply-to-my-thread-about-having-a-problem-at-times-eating-50-gr-of-protein.5307/post-68919>

Some times our ability to adapt up to the protein amounts also takes time.......but it can happen. Just keep falling forward in 2013.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Need to Keep Cold All the Time?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mel.boguski@gmail.com — 2013-01-01T16:18:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-68930>

Hi you guys,
in order to become cold adapted, can you basically live like a warm adapted human (except for lights and diet) but with cold showers/baths?
Or is it important to start living and sleeping in a colder and colder environment?
Should one always avoid warm situations or warm showers, say? Would that undo cold adaptation?
What about saunas? It would be so much easier to get your skin good and cold painlessly if you heated yourself up first. Is that a no-no?
Are cold showers not as effective as cold baths? Do I have to add ice? It seems like such an inconvenience.
Thanks all!

### freesia — 2013-01-01T22:31:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-68952>

> **Mela said**
>
> Hi you guys,
> in order to become cold adapted, can you basically live like a warm adapted human (except for lights and diet) but with cold showers/baths?
> Or is it important to start living and sleeping in a colder and colder environment?
> Should one always avoid warm situations or warm showers, say? Would that undo cold adaptation?
> What about saunas? It would be so much easier to get your skin good and cold painlessly if you heated yourself up first. Is that a no-no?
> Are cold showers not as effective as cold baths? Do I have to add ice? It seems like such an inconvenience.
> Thanks all!

Hi Mela,
My interpretation of how I'm "cold adapted" is that now, immediately my skin is stimulated by cold (or even when I'm anticipating cold) my body warms up in response to that stimulus. Cold adaptation like Dr Kruse lays out in his CT blog series comes about by regularly getting your skin temperature to between 50 and 55 F. You'll need ice to achieve that indoors I think (unless your tap water gets down around 50F or less?).
Regular exposure to cold will mean after a while it will take less to make you feel warm. (I still take warm showers but anything much above lukewarm feels stifling.) I slept with less blankets on than I ever have over winter, didn't need to wear gloves or a winter coat...or a hat even once.

I got cold adapted with face plunges, then showers, then baths...adding more and more ice until my water was at 46 F. I'm taking a 45 minute bath 3 times per week at the moment, but over winter when it was easier to get the water that cold, I was bathing every day.

I don't know where you're at healthwise, but I was pretty bad. Housebound. Any inconvenience for me personally, is outweighed x 1000 by the fact I'm doing so well. Plus I had phenomenal support....from my family, and from the trailblazers here.

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-02T05:01:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-68990>

Ann, that was a very helpful reply. I had some of those questions too. I'm just looking to check a few things out before I go do another face plunge. I did it earlier today and to my surprise found it very difficult even in tap cold water that is really quite warm. I've been doing some (warmish) cold baths off and on since last April and do find that I don't need the blankets I used to and got through much of winter with what had previously been summer blankets. But I've never tried the face plunges or the ice before. So I'm going back to the beginning of Jack's advice and starting with the face plunges.

### freesia — 2013-01-02T05:34:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-68991>

Aussie nana...I think the "trick" is just to (as Dr Kruse says) keep falling forward. The last thing you want to do is try and match someone elses rate of progress and turn yourself off CT in the process. Cold adaptation is too special a prize to rush for and risk breakage.
If it feels cold to you, that's where you need to be. If recovery is rough don't aim for more the following day just aim to do the same temperature (whether it's 70F or 50F) and hope for an easier recovery. That's still moving forward.
You're in summertime now like me? That's a GREAT time to start I think.....although if you went through winter with summer blankets it sounds like you've already made some decent progress. Yay!

There's something I didn't mention to the original poster and that is that I think you need to go into cold adaptation with a certain amount of faith. So long as you're consistent with the cold water, and you decrease the temperature, it's a naturally evolving process. You manage it to the best of your ability day by day. If I went somewhere heated I didn't think too much about it...I just removed a layer or two until I was comfortable. It meant, from a few weeks into CT, all winter I was wearing a bit less ...sometimes a lot less (!) than people around me.
Get used to wearing lots of layers of lightweight natural fibre for maximum convenience and comfort.

...By the time you're out the other end of winter......be prepared to want to wear your birthday suit and a smile and not much else! LOL.

Anne.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-03T00:05:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-69090>

Anne is spot on.........

### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-07T02:31:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-69710>

I'm not sure if others have this experience but when its really hot here (100+F) and everyone is complaining I find myself tolerating the heat better than most people. Instead of turning the air con on as soon as it hits 28 (82F) I don't until I'm sitting and its over 32 (90F). I'm able to keep working in the heat without even thinking of it much as long as I'm not in the sun and at risk of burning. Has anyone else had this experience?

Also I went to buy meat from a wholesale shop that is kept at freezer temperatures. Everyone else had to wear thermal jackets. I went in and spent 20 minutes in there and was really cold after about 5 minutes, but at 10 minutes I warmed up and was fine till it came time to leave. Then when everyone was complaining about the 100F heat outside I was nicely cool and managed the transition.

This is rather fun.

### kathylu — 2013-01-07T03:06:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-69714>

Same for me. I tolerated the summer heat last summer better than I ever have before. People are saying that 2012 was the hottest year on record in the US. I didn't particularly notice!

### ashryn — 2013-01-07T06:43:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-69721>

> **Aussie nana said**
>
> I'm not sure if others have this experience but when its really hot here (100+F) and everyone is complaining I find myself tolerating the heat better than most people. Instead of turning the air con on as soon as it hits 28 (82F) I don't until I'm sitting and its over 32 (90F). I'm able to keep working in the heat without even thinking of it much as long as I'm not in the sun and at risk of burning. Has anyone else had this experience?
> Also I went to buy meat from a wholesale shop that is kept at freezer temperatures. Everyone else had to wear thermal jackets. I went in and spent 20 minutes in there and was really cold after about 5 minutes, but at 10 minutes I warmed up and was fine till it came time to leave. Then when everyone was complaining about the 100F heat outside I was nicely cool and managed the transition.
> This is rather fun.

Ooh! Where is that place? maybe they'll give me a part time job so i can go hang out in their freezer!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-07T12:47:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-69746>

When you use CT you gain the ability.......to tolerate heat well because you become able to release free heat easy.

### freesia — 2013-01-07T15:32:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-to-keep-cold-all-the-time.5310/post-69768>

Makes perfect sense. Thermoadaptive fitness?

We've had some extreme temps here over Christmas and New Year. An uncharacteristic high followed by a sharp cold change - lotsa big electrical storms....which is big fun while camping with kids in a tent by the way!

...Usually I'd be completely wiped out by weather antics like that but my new found (and much cherished!) energy hasn't flagged at all. So empowering.

### lauren_r — 2013-01-01T23:27:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacon.5315/post-68955>

I was wondering if everyone eats Free Range Bacon?
Free range bacon is pretty expensive in Australia, more than any other meat/seafood source. I do only eat grass fed, free range etc, however
the amount of bacon i would like to include for protein in my diet makes it a strain to include a lot. Due to conditions of pig farming, i think the stress/inflammation
in the meat from traditional supplies is prob not worth it...thoughts???

Also what are thoughts on non free range lard? SHould it be avoided due to stress/inflammation in meat from horrible conditions?
thanks

### furface — 2013-01-02T00:54:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacon.5315/post-68957>

I eat regular, conventional bacon, but I check the label and try and find one with no added sugar. Maple Leaf reduced salt bacon has no added sugar. I think if we worry about every piddly ass detail in every single thing we eat or do, the stress of worrying will be more hazardous than any miniscule little damage whatever "problems" what we were worried about would, or even could cause.

I recently did try some double smoked free range bacon from Chilliwack B.C. but for $12.99 lb. I should have gotten the whole pig!! It was good, but not $12.99 lb. worth good!

As far as the lard goes, I found a local butcher shop that sells Grass-Fed beef tallow for $5.99 for a 2.5" x 5" x 1.25" slab.

### Thor — 2013-01-06T01:32:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacon.5315/post-69553>

.
I'm confused how you would say Nitrites are safe??? Nitrites have been clearly shown not safe - they cause cancer and disrupt hormones.

Russell L. Blaylock, M.D has shown chemicals can damage a critical part of the brain known to control hormones, leading to endocrine problems. They are called excitotoxins and include chemicals like; Artificial colors (FD&C colors) Sodium nitrite and nitrate, BHT (butylated hydroxytohicne) Saccharin, MSG (monosodium glutamate), Aspartame Caffeine, Propylene glycol, Sulfites (especially sodium bisulfite) Sulfur dioxide, BVO (Brominated Vegetable Oil)

sodium nitrite reacts with the stomach acids to produce nitrosamines - one of the deadliest known carcinogens.

The USDA actually tried to ban sodium nitrite in the 1970's, but was stopped by the meat processing industry - however the dangers of Nitrites were discovered in 1956.

Pubmed alone has several thousand research papers pointing to the dangers. But it's safe?

Pancreatic cancer isn't the only negative side effect of consuming processed meats such as hot dogs. Leukemia also skyrockets by 700% following the consumption of hot dogs and bacon. (Preston-Martin, S. et al. "N-nitroso compounds and childhood brain tumors: A case-control study." Cancer Res. 1982; 42:5240-5.)

Effect of processed and red meat on endogenous nitrosation and DNA damage

http://carcin.oxfordjournals.org/content/30/8/1402.full

In fact pancreatic CA incidence is over 500% since the USDA went to a 60% carb stance.........I dont buy the link at all. The science and data are weak.........Blaylock is right about MSG and some others but he is an alarmist on others.......for the revenue stream in my view.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-01-06T12:51:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacon.5315/post-69597>

yesterday in IPMG i wrote this about two of the chemicals you mentioned above: Butylated hydroxyanisole (BHA) is an antioxidant consisting of a mixture of two isomeric organic compounds, 2-tert-butyl-4-hydroxyanisole and 3-tert-butyl-4-hydroxyanisole.The primary use for BHA is as an antioxidant and preservative in food, food packaging, animal feed, cosmetics, rubber, and petroleum products and some meds like statins! The US National Institutes of Health report that BHA is reasonably anticipated to be a human carcinogen based on evidence of carcinogenicity in experimental animals. In particular, when administered in high doses as part of their diet, BHA causes papillomas and squamous cell carcinomas of the forestomach in rats and Syrian golden hamster. In Cali it is a listed carcinogen for humans but no where else. It is also OK in mice studies. Butylated hydroxytoluene (BHT), also known as butylhydroxytoluene, is a fat-soluble, organic compound, chemically a derivative of phenol, that is useful for its antioxidant properties. It has amazing properties against herpes virus so it is not all bad! BHT is used in fluids/soft solids to prevent oxidation. I use BHT for many people with certain viral conditions. It is not a bad drug. BHA is more controversial but based upon what I have read not too bad compared to most other neolithic agents. I would avoid BHA but I have zero issues with BHT.

### Jonathin — 2013-01-06T17:18:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bacon.5315/post-69637>

Pork tastes great no doubt. My wife just salt cured jowls from the half a hog we got from a friend. What a pig eats does matter. Truly pastured pork is practically non-existent as the productivity is low unless the producer has the perfect land situation to provide plenty of calories such as an oak woodlot. Most pigs are fed plenty of industrially produced agricultural commodities. Anything that is larger than a small well-managed farm is most likely an ecological nightmare. Compare that to something like grass-finished beef and you are talking about a world of difference in multiple ways.

.

Most Vegans eat a ton of soy based foods - tofu to edamame. Nearly all baby formulas have soy based protien as an ingredient. Baby boys who ingest soy will end up chemically castrated before their first birthday. Over time those boys will have small under-developed testicles and a smaller than normal penis - sometimes referred to as "micro-penis" yikes! The average penis will be under 2 inches in a short period of evolution. Sperm counts will continue to fall. and so on.........

Here's some proof: http://www.fws.gov/chesapeakebay/pdf/endocrine.pdf
Now scientists are connecting the dots with evidence of increasing abnormalities among humans, particularly large increases in numbers of genital deformities among newborn boys. For example, up to 10 percent of boys are now born with undescended testicles, which can cause problems up to and including surgical castration. Now up to 2 percent of boys in the United States are born with hypospadias, in which the urethra exits the penis improperly, such as at the base rather than the tip. Translates to peeing out the base of your penis.

Keep chowin' down on that vegen food! It's not as healthy as those tree-huggin' fruit loops like to think.

# Thread: Hello from Western Australia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-western-australia.5318/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Aussie Nana — 2013-01-02T04:53:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-western-australia.5318/post-68987>

Hi all
I'm a 62 year old woman currently living in Western Australia where we've just had a heat wave with temperatures from 37 to 42 C (98-105 F). I've had continuous health issues all my life that the doctors would disregard and tell me there was nothing wrong with me other than a mildly elevated lab that would change from time to time. As I've grown older the diagnoses changed from nothing to various autoimmune diagnoses which changed with the years. I've been searching for health for years and I hope I've found what I need here.

I'm currently eating a shellfish breakfast and paleo the rest of the time. I'm sugar free, grain free, legume and solanum free (except for a blip over Christmas). Now back on the wagon and eat no processed foods.

I've been reading and reading and reading Jack's blog and its making more and more sense, so I'm doing more and more. Now off to check the criteria for CT.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-western-australia.5318/post-68989>

A big welcome from another Aussie! You will find so many really helpful/smart people here to help if you need it. This has been and continues to be an amazing journey

# Thread: Does anyone have a foolproof way to......
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-have-a-foolproof-way-to.5321/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 14
